topics.title: Propositional Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES (INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Propositional Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC (Sentential Logic / Statement Logic)** is the branch of mathematical logic that deals with **propositions** (statements that are either true or false, but not both) and the **logical connectives** used to combine or modify them. It is the foundational framework upon which all of digital circuit design, programming language semantics, and formal verification in computer science is built.

A **proposition** (or **statement**) is a declarative sentence that has a definite truth value — either **TRUE (T)**, denoted by **1**, or **FALSE (F)**, denoted by **0**.

**KTU Classification of Propositions:**
- **Atomic Proposition (Primitive):** A proposition that cannot be broken down into smaller propositions. Example: $p$: "5 is prime."
- **Compound Proposition (Molecular):** A proposition constructed by combining two or more atomic propositions using **logical connectives**. Example: $p \land q$: "5 is prime AND 7 is odd."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** The five fundamental logical connectives that you MUST memorize are **Negation (¬)**, **Conjunction (∧)**, **Disjunction (∨)**, **Implication (→)**, and **Biconditional (↔)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of propositional logic as the **"switches and wires" of pure thought**:

| Real World | Propositional Logic | Intuition |
|------------|--------------------|-----------|
| A light switch | A proposition $p$ | It is either ON (True) or OFF (False) |
| Two switches in series | Conjunction $p \land q$ | Light glows only if **BOTH** are ON |
| Two switches in parallel | Disjunction $p \lor q$ | Light glows if **EITHER** is ON |
| Master override switch | Negation $\neg p$ | Flips the state of the switch |
| A "Cause → Effect" relationship | Implication $p \rightarrow q$ | If cause happens, effect must follow |
| A two-way switch | Biconditional $p \leftrightarrow q$ | Both switches are in the **SAME** position |

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Trick:** A proposition is like a coin lying flat on a table. It is **either** Heads (T) **or** Tails (F) — it cannot be standing on its edge. Sentences like "What time is it?" or "$x + 3 = 7$" are **NOT propositions** because they are either questions or have variable truth values.

## 1.3 Sentences That Are NOT Propositions

> [!CAUTION]
> The following categories of sentences are **NOT** valid propositions in KTU examinations:
> 1. **Questions:** "Are you hungry?"
> 2. **Commands/Imperatives:** "Close the door."
> 3. **Exclamations:** "What a beautiful day!"
> 4. **Paradoxes:** "This sentence is false." (cannot be assigned a consistent T/F value)
> 5. **Sentences with variables:** "$x + y > 10$" (depends on $x$ and $y$)

## 1.4 The Five Primitive Logical Connectives

Let $p$ and $q$ be two propositions. The standard KTU connectives are:

| Symbol | Name | Common English Reading | Mathematical Read As |
|--------|------|------------------------|----------------------|
| $\neg p$ | Negation | NOT $p$ | "It is not the case that $p$" |
| $p \land q$ | Conjunction | $p$ AND $q$ | "Both $p$ and $q$ are true" |
| $p \lor q$ | Disjunction (inclusive) | $p$ OR $q$ | "At least one of $p, q$ is true" |
| $p \rightarrow q$ | Implication (Conditional) | IF $p$ THEN $q$ | "$p$ implies $q$" |
| $p \leftrightarrow q$ | Biconditional | $p$ IF AND ONLY IF $q$ | "$p$ and $q$ have the same truth value" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention Warning:** In KTU exams, unless explicitly stated otherwise, the **OR ($\lor$)** operator is the **inclusive OR**, meaning $p \lor q$ is true even when both $p$ and $q$ are true. This is the standard mathematical convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Truth Table — The Heart of Propositional Logic

A **truth table** is a tabular representation that lists **all possible combinations of truth values** for the atomic propositions and computes the truth value of the resulting compound proposition. For $n$ atomic propositions, the truth table has exactly $2^n$ rows.

### 2.1.1 Negation ($\neg p$)

| $p$ | $\neg p$ |
|:---:|:--------:|
| T | F |
| F | T |

**Logic:** Negation simply **inverts** (flips) the truth value. If $p$ is true, $\neg p$ is false, and vice versa.

### 2.1.2 Conjunction ($p \land q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \land q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------:|
| T | T | **T** |
| T | F | F |
| F | T | F |
| F | F | F |

**Logic:** True **ONLY** when **BOTH** $p$ and $q$ are true. This is the most restrictive connective.

### 2.1.3 Disjunction ($p \lor q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \lor q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:----------:|
| T | T | **T** |
| T | F | **T** |
| F | T | **T** |
| F | F | F |

**Logic:** False **ONLY** when **BOTH** $p$ and $q$ are false. Otherwise true (inclusive OR).

### 2.1.4 Implication / Conditional ($p \rightarrow q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|
| T | T | **T** |
| T | F | **F** |
| F | T | **T** |
| F | F | **T** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU CONCEPT — The "Material Implication":** A conditional statement $p \rightarrow q$ is **false ONLY when the antecedent ($p$) is true and the consequent ($q$) is false**. In all other three cases, it is TRUE. This often confuses students because in everyday English, "If it rains, I'll bring an umbrella" implies a causal connection. But in logic, the implication is a **purely formal** truth-functional operation — there is no causal requirement.

**KTU Exam Terminology:**
- $p$ is called the **hypothesis / antecedent / premise**
- $q$ is called the **conclusion / consequent**
- $p \rightarrow q$ is also called a **conditional statement**

**Related Conditional Statements (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**

| Name | Symbolic Form | Description |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| Converse | $q \rightarrow p$ | Swap hypothesis and conclusion |
| Inverse | $\neg p \rightarrow \neg q$ | Negate both |
| Contrapositive | $\neg q \rightarrow \neg p$ | Negate AND swap |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Gold:** A conditional $p \rightarrow q$ is **logically equivalent to its contrapositive $\neg q \rightarrow \neg p$**, but it is **NOT** equivalent to its converse or inverse. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

### 2.1.5 Biconditional ($p \leftrightarrow q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \leftrightarrow q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:--------------------:|
| T | T | **T** |
| T | F | F |
| F | T | F |
| F | F | **T** |

**Logic:** True when $p$ and $q$ have the **SAME** truth value. Equivalent to $(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$.

## 2.2 Order of Precedence (Operator Priority)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU requires students to know and apply the order of precedence** when evaluating complex compound propositions. From **highest** to **lowest** priority:

$$\neg \quad > \quad \land \quad > \quad \lor \quad > \quad \rightarrow \quad > \quad \leftrightarrow$$

**Memory Trick:** **"Not** *and* **Or** *Then* **Iff"** — Negation binds tightest, biconditional is the loosest.

## 2.3 Classification of Compound Propositions (HIGH-YIELD)

For any compound proposition $P(p, q, r, \ldots)$:

| Classification | Definition | Truth Table Column |
|----------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Tautology** ($\top$ or $1$) | **Always TRUE** for every assignment | All T's |
| **Contradiction** ($\bot$ or $0$) | **Always FALSE** for every assignment | All F's |
| **Contingency** | Sometimes true, sometimes false (depends on inputs) | Mixed T's and F's |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A **single implication** $p \rightarrow q$ is a **contingency**, not a tautology! It fails when $p = T, q = F$. A common student error is to call every "obvious" statement a tautology.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **condensed reference of all high-yield identities** in Propositional Logic. These are examinable directly in KTU 2024 scheme examinations.

| # | Identity / Law | Equivalence (Symbolic Form) |
|:-:|----------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Identity Law** | $p \land T \equiv p$, $\quad p \lor F \equiv p$ |
| 2 | **Domination Law** | $p \lor T \equiv T$, $\quad p \land F \equiv F$ |
| 3 | **Idempotent Law** | $p \lor p \equiv p$, $\quad p \land p \equiv p$ |
| 4 | **Double Negation Law** | $\neg(\neg p) \equiv p$ |
| 5 | **Commutative Law** | $p \land q \equiv q \land p$, $\quad p \lor q \equiv q \lor p$ |
| 6 | **Associative Law** | $(p \land q) \land r \equiv p \land (q \land r)$, $\quad$ (similar for $\lor$) |
| 7 | **Distributive Law** | $p \land (q \lor r) \equiv (p \land q) \lor (p \land r)$ |
| 8 | **Distributive Law (OR over AND)** | $p \lor (q \land r) \equiv (p \lor q) \land (p \lor r)$ |
| 9 | **De Morgan's Law — AND** | $\neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg p \lor \neg q$ |
| 10 | **De Morgan's Law — OR** | $\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q$ |
| 11 | **Absorption Law** | $p \lor (p \land q) \equiv p$, $\quad p \land (p \lor q) \equiv p$ |
| 12 | **Negation / Complement Law** | $p \lor \neg p \equiv T$, $\quad p \land \neg p \equiv F$ |
| 13 | **Implication Law** | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q$ |
| 14 | **Contrapositive Law** | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg q \rightarrow \neg p$ |
| 15 | **Biconditional Expansion** | $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$ |
| 16 | **Biconditional Equivalence** | $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \land q) \lor (\neg p \land \neg q)$ |
| 17 | **Exportation Law** | $(p \land q) \rightarrow r \equiv p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow r)$ |
| 18 | **Tautology as Implication** | $\top \equiv p \rightarrow p$, $\quad$ $\bot \equiv p \land \neg p$ |

## 2.5 Logical Equivalence — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two compound propositions $P$ and $Q$ (involving the same atomic propositions) are said to be **logically equivalent**, written $P \equiv Q$, if and only if they have **identical truth tables** — i.e., $P \leftrightarrow Q$ is a **tautology**.

## 2.6 Principle of Duality

> [!NOTE]
> The **dual** of a compound proposition $P$, denoted $P^d$, is obtained by replacing every $\land$ with $\lor$, every $\lor$ with $\land$, every $T$ with $F$, and every $F$ with $T$ (the atomic propositions remain unchanged). **If $P \equiv Q$ is a tautology, then $P^d \equiv Q^d$ is also a tautology.** This is a high-yield KTU theory question.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Logic Design:** Every combinational logic circuit (adders, multiplexers, decoders) is built from NAND/NOR gates which are direct implementations of the connectives $\land, \lor, \neg$. Truth tables in propositional logic **are** the truth tables in digital electronics.
- **Programming Language Semantics:** Short-circuit evaluation of `&&` and `||` in C/Java/Python mirrors the truth-functional behavior of $\land$ and $\lor$.
- **Artificial Intelligence:** Knowledge representation and rule-based expert systems use propositional logic for inference.
- **Software Verification:** Formal methods (model checking) use propositional logic to verify that software/hardware satisfies its specification.
- **Database Query Optimization:** SQL query conditions are essentially propositional logic formulas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building a Truth Table for a Compound Statement

**Problem:** Construct the truth table for: $(p \rightarrow q) \land (\neg p \lor q)$

**Step 1 — Identify atomic propositions:** $p$ and $q$. Therefore, we need $2^2 = 4$ rows.

**Step 2 — Determine required columns:** $p$, $q$, $p \rightarrow q$, $\neg p$, $\neg p \lor q$, and the final column $(p \rightarrow q) \land (\neg p \lor q)$.

**Step 3 — Compute column by column following operator precedence:**

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg p \lor q$ | $(p \rightarrow q) \land (\neg p \lor q)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:--------:|:--------------:|:------------------------------------------:|
| T | T | T | F | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | F | F |
| F | T | T | T | T | T |
| F | F | T | T | T | T |

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:** The final column is **NOT** all T's and **NOT** all F's (it has three T's and one F). Therefore, this compound proposition is a **CONTINGENCY**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Compare row 1 and row 3 — both are T, showing this expression behaves like $q$ itself. Indeed, we can prove $(p \rightarrow q) \land (\neg p \lor q) \equiv q$ using logical equivalences.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proving De Morgan's Law using Truth Table

**Theorem:** $\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q$

**Step 1 — Construct columns for $p$, $q$, $p \lor q$, $\neg(p \lor q)$, $\neg p$, $\neg q$, $\neg p \land \neg q$:**

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \lor q$ | $\neg(p \lor q)$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg q$ | $\neg p \land \neg q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:----------:|:----------------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------------------:|
| T | T | T | **F** | F | F | **F** |
| T | F | T | **F** | F | T | **F** |
| F | T | T | **F** | T | F | **F** |
| F | F | F | **T** | T | T | **T** |

**Step 2 — Compare columns 4 and 7:** The entries are **IDENTICAL** in every row (F, F, F, T).

**Step 3 — Conclude:** Since the truth tables of $\neg(p \lor q)$ and $\neg p \land \neg q$ are identical, the propositions are **logically equivalent**. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** When asked to "verify using a truth table" in KTU, you must show **ALL intermediate columns** with arrow indicators explaining how each column is derived from previous ones. The examiner awards step-marks for this.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Algebraic Proof of $p \rightarrow (q \lor r) \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \lor (p \rightarrow r)$

This is a **distributive-like property of implication over disjunction** — a classic KTU problem.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p \rightarrow q) \lor (p \rightarrow r) &\equiv (\neg p \lor q) \lor (\neg p \lor r) & &\text{(Implication Law: rows 13 of Cheat Sheet)} \\
&\equiv \neg p \lor (q \lor \neg p) \lor r & &\text{(Commutative + Associative Laws: rows 5, 6)} \\
&\equiv \neg p \lor \neg p \lor q \lor r & &\text{(Reordering)} \\
&\equiv \neg p \lor (q \lor r) & &\text{(Idempotent Law: row 3)} \\
&\equiv p \rightarrow (q \lor r) & &\text{(Implication Law applied in reverse)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $p \rightarrow (q \lor r) \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \lor (p \rightarrow r)$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the asymmetry:** Implication distributes over $\lor$ on the **right side** but **NOT** on the left side. The statement $p \rightarrow (q \land r) \not\equiv (p \rightarrow q) \land (p \rightarrow r)$ is false — students often confuse this.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Algebraic Proof of De Morgan's Law (AND version)

**Theorem:** $\neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg p \lor \neg q$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg p \lor \neg q &\equiv \neg p \lor \neg q & &\text{(Given — start with the RHS)} \\
&\equiv \neg(\neg(\neg p)) \lor \neg(\neg q) & &\text{(Double Negation: row 4)} \\
&\equiv \neg(\neg p) \rightarrow \neg q & &\text{(Implication Law: } a \rightarrow b \equiv \neg a \lor b \text{, set } a = \neg p, b = q) \\
&\equiv (\neg q) \rightarrow (\neg p) & &\text{(Contrapositive swap)} \\
&\equiv \neg(\neg q) \lor \neg(\neg p) & &\text{(Implication Law expanded)} \\
&\equiv q \lor p & &\text{(Double Negation: row 4)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Hmm, this path is getting circular. Let us use the **standard textbook approach** with a direct chain of equivalences on the LHS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg(p \land q) &\equiv \neg p \lor \neg q \\
\text{We can prove it by showing both are equivalent to a third canonical form.} \\
(p \land q) &\text{ is true iff both } p, q \text{ are true.} \\
\neg(p \land q) &\text{ is true iff at least one of } p, q \text{ is false.} \\
\neg p \lor \neg q &\text{ is true iff } \neg p \text{ is true OR } \neg q \text{ is true,} \\
&\text{i.e., at least one of } p, q \text{ is false.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since both are true under **exactly the same conditions**, they are logically equivalent. For a rigorous algebraic proof, we invoke the truth table proof already shown in **Section 3.2**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Classifying a Compound Statement

**Problem:** Classify the statement $(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$ as a tautology, contradiction, or contingency.

**Step 1 — Construct the truth table:**

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg p \lor q$ | $(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:--------:|:--------------:|:---------------------------------------------------:|
| T | T | T | F | T | **T** |
| T | F | F | F | F | **T** |
| F | T | T | T | T | **T** |
| F | F | T | T | T | **T** |

**Step 2 — Observe the final column:** It is **ALL T's** (T, T, T, T).

**Step 3 — Conclude:** This is a **TAUTOLOGY**. This is precisely the **Implication Law** ($p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q$) verified empirically.

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Translating English to Logic

**Problem:** Translate: "If it is not sunny and it is cold, then I will not go swimming." Let:
- $p$: "It is sunny"
- $q$: "It is cold"
- $r$: "I will go swimming"

**Step 1 — Identify the logical structure:** "It is not sunny" = $\neg p$, "and it is cold" = $\land q$, "then I will not go swimming" = $\rightarrow \neg r$.

**Step 2 — Form the formula:** $(\neg p \land q) \rightarrow \neg r$

**Step 3 — (Optional) Apply Exportation Law:** $p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow r) \equiv (p \land q) \rightarrow r$. So $(\neg p \land q) \rightarrow \neg r$ can be rewritten as $\neg p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow \neg r)$ — meaning "If it's not sunny, then if it's cold, I won't swim."

## 3.7 Worked Example 7 — The Contrapositive Trap

**Problem:** State the contrapositive of: "If I am in Kerala, then it rains." Let $p$: "I am in Kerala", $q$: "It rains."

**Original:** $p \rightarrow q$

**Contrapositive:** $\neg q \rightarrow \neg p$, i.e., "If it is not raining, then I am not in Kerala."

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write the **converse** "If it rains, then I am in Kerala" as the contrapositive. This is logically **INCORRECT** and will cost marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Logical Connective Hierarchy and Equivalences

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Atomic Propositions p, q, r] --> B[Compound Propositions]
    B --> C1[NOT - Negation]
    B --> C2[AND - Conjunction]
    B --> C3[OR - Disjunction]
    B --> C4[IF THEN - Implication]
    B --> C5[IF AND ONLY IF - Biconditional]
    C1 --> D1[Output: p, q, r negated]
    C2 --> D2[Output: p AND q]
    C3 --> D3[Output: p OR q]
    C4 --> D4[Output: p IMPLIES q]
    C5 --> D5[Output: p IFF q]
    C4 --> E1[Equivalent to: NOT p OR q]
    C5 --> E2[Equivalent to: p AND q OR NOT p AND NOT q]
    C4 --> E3[Contrapositive: NOT q IMPLIES NOT p]
    C1 --> F1[De Morgan: NOT p AND q = NOT p OR NOT q]
    C1 --> F2[De Morgan: NOT p OR q = NOT p AND NOT q]
    D1 --> G1[Tautology: all TRUE]
    D1 --> G2[Contradiction: all FALSE]
    D1 --> G3[Contingency: mixed]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Truth Table Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify atomic propositions] --> S2[Step 2: Compute 2 to the n rows]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: List all T F combinations]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply precedence - Negation first]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Then AND connectives]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Then OR connectives]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Then Implication]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Then Biconditional last]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Read final column]
    S9 --> R1{All TRUE?}
    S9 --> R2{All FALSE?}
    R1 -- YES --> CLASS1[TAUTOLOGY]
    R1 -- NO --> R3{Mixed?}
    R2 -- YES --> CLASS2[CONTRADICTION]
    R3 -- YES --> CLASS3[CONTINGENCY]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Conditional Statement Family

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Conditional: p IMPLIES q] --> B[Converse: q IMPLIES p]
    A --> C[Inverse: NOT p IMPLIES NOT q]
    A --> D[Contrapositive: NOT q IMPLIES NOT p]
    A -. EQUIVALENT .-> D
    B -. NOT EQUIVALENT .-> C
    style A fill:#ffebcc
    style D fill:#cce5ff
    style B fill:#ffcccc
    style C fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Logic Gate Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Input: Proposition p] --> G1[NOT Gate]
    Q[Input: Proposition q] --> G1
    P --> G2[AND Gate]
    Q --> G2
    P --> G3[OR Gate]
    Q --> G3
    P --> G4[IMPLIES Gate]
    Q --> G4
    P --> G5[BICONDITIONAL Gate]
    Q --> G5
    G1 --> O1[Output: NOT p]
    G2 --> O2[Output: p AND q]
    G3 --> O3[Output: p OR q]
    G4 --> O4[Output: p IMPLIES q]
    G5 --> O5[Output: p IFF q]
    style G1 fill:#fff2cc
    style G2 fill:#d5e8d4
    style G3 fill:#d5e8d4
    style G4 fill:#dae8fc
    style G5 fill:#dae8fc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each of the five logical connectives corresponds directly to a fundamental **logic gate** used in digital circuit design. The **IMPLIES Gate** is implemented using a NOT gate on input $p$ followed by an OR gate with $q$ — this is precisely the Implication Law $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q$ realized in hardware. The **BICONDITIONAL Gate** is built from an XNOR gate (exclusive-NOR), which is the equality comparator at the bit level.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions in ESE (End Semester Evaluation) carry **3 marks** each and require concise, definition-style answers. The word limit is typically **50–80 words**.

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Tautology, (ii) Contradiction, (iii) Contingency.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Tautology:** A compound proposition that is **always true** for every possible truth value assignment of its atomic propositions.

*Example:* $p \lor \neg p$ (Law of Excluded Middle). The truth table of this expression is:
- $p = T, \neg p = F \Rightarrow p \lor \neg p = T$
- $p = F, \neg p = T \Rightarrow p \lor \neg p = T$

**(ii) Contradiction:** A compound proposition that is **always false** for every possible truth value assignment of its atomic propositions.

*Example:* $p \land \neg p$. Truth table:
- $p = T, \neg p = F \Rightarrow p \land \neg p = F$
- $p = F, \neg p = T \Rightarrow p \land \neg p = F$

**(iii) Contingency:** A compound proposition that is **sometimes true and sometimes false**, depending on the truth values of its atomic propositions.

*Example:* $p \rightarrow q$. When $p = T, q = F$, the value is F; in all other cases it is T. Hence it is a contingency.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` State and prove De Morgan's Laws in propositional logic.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**De Morgan's Laws:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Law 1: } & \neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg p \lor \neg q \\
\text{Law 2: } & \neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof of Law 2** (using truth table):

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \lor q$ | $\neg(p \lor q)$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg q$ | $\neg p \land \neg q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:----------:|:----------------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------------------:|
| T | T | T | **F** | F | F | **F** |
| T | F | T | **F** | F | T | **F** |
| F | T | T | **F** | T | F | **F** |
| F | F | F | **T** | T | T | **T** |

Columns 4 and 7 are identical. Therefore $\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question in ESE carries **14 marks** and typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The exam provides an internal choice — you must answer **ONE** out of the two alternatives. Both alternatives below are constructed to be of **equal difficulty** to match KTU 2024 standards.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Construct the truth table for the compound proposition $(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$. Simplify it and state its standard name. (7 marks)

**(b)** Show using algebraic laws of logic that $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \land q) \lor (\neg p \land \neg q)$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to A(a):**

We need to construct a truth table for $(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$. There are 2 atomic propositions, so the table has $2^2 = 4$ rows.

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $q \rightarrow p$ | $(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------------------------------------:|
| T | T | T | T | **T** |
| T | F | F | T | **F** |
| F | T | T | F | **F** |
| F | F | T | T | **T** |

**Step-by-step column derivation:**
- **Column 3** ($p \rightarrow q$): F only when $p=T, q=F$. All others T.
- **Column 4** ($q \rightarrow p$): F only when $q=T, p=F$. All others T.
- **Column 5** (final AND): T only when both column 3 AND column 4 are T. That happens at rows 1 and 4 only.

**Simplification:**

Notice that column 5 is **T when $p$ and $q$ have the SAME truth value** and **F when they differ**. This is precisely the **Biconditional** $p \leftrightarrow q$.

Therefore:
$$(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p) \equiv p \leftrightarrow q$$

**Standard Name:** This expression is the canonical expansion of the **Biconditional** connective. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to A(b):**

We must show that $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \land q) \lor (\neg p \land \neg q)$.

Starting from the LHS and applying logical equivalences step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p \leftrightarrow q &\equiv (p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p) & &\text{(Biconditional expansion, row 15)} \\
&\equiv (\neg p \lor q) \land (\neg q \lor p) & &\text{(Implication Law applied to both, row 13)} \\
&\equiv (q \lor \neg p) \land (p \lor \neg q) & &\text{(Commutative Law, row 5)} \\
&\equiv (q \land p) \lor (q \land \neg q) \lor (\neg p \land p) \lor (\neg p \land \neg q) & &\text{(Distributive Law, row 8)} \\
&\equiv (p \land q) \lor F \lor F \lor (\neg p \land \neg q) & &\text{(Negation Law, row 12)} \\
&\equiv (p \land q) \lor (\neg p \land \neg q) & &\text{(Identity Law, row 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \land q) \lor (\neg p \land \neg q)$. $\blacksquare$ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Without using a truth table, prove that $\neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \land \neg q$ using the laws of propositional logic. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine whether $(\neg p \lor q) \land (\neg q \lor p)$ is a tautology, contradiction, or contingency. Justify your answer with a complete truth table. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to B(a):**

We begin with the LHS and apply the Implication Law, De Morgan's Law, and Double Negation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg(p \rightarrow q) &\equiv \neg(\neg p \lor q) & &\text{(Implication Law, row 13: } p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q) \\
&\equiv \neg(\neg p) \land \neg q & &\text{(De Morgan's Law, row 10)} \\
&\equiv p \land \neg q & &\text{(Double Negation Law, row 4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \land \neg q$. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** This is the famous "negation of an implication" identity. Memorize it — it is used in **every** KTU module from inference rules to predicate logic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B(b):**

We construct the truth table for $P = (\neg p \lor q) \land (\neg q \lor p)$. Since there are 2 atomic propositions, we have 4 rows.

| $p$ | $q$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg q$ | $\neg p \lor q$ | $\neg q \lor p$ | $P = (\neg p \lor q) \land (\neg q \lor p)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:-------------------------------------------:|
| T | T | F | F | T | T | **T** |
| T | F | F | T | F | T | **F** |
| F | T | T | F | T | F | **F** |
| F | F | T | T | T | T | **T** |

**Step-by-step column derivation:**
- **Column 3** ($\neg p$): Invert column 1.
- **Column 4** ($\neg q$): Invert column 2.
- **Column 5** ($\neg p \lor q$): T except when both $\neg p$ and $q$ are F. (Row 2 is the only F.)
- **Column 6** ($\neg q \lor p$): T except when both $\neg q$ and $p$ are F. (Row 3 is the only F.)
- **Column 7** (final AND): T only when both column 5 AND column 6 are T simultaneously. This occurs in rows 1 and 4.

**Observation:** The final column is **T, F, F, T** — it is neither all T's (tautology) nor all F's (contradiction).

**Conclusion:** The proposition $(\neg p \lor q) \land (\neg q \lor p)$ is a **CONTINGENCY**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This expression is actually equivalent to $p \leftrightarrow q$, which is exactly what we proved in Question A(a). Hence the same truth table structure. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Propositional Logic Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the operator precedence** when writing the truth table: Students often compute $p \rightarrow q \land r$ incorrectly as $(p \rightarrow q) \land r$ instead of $p \rightarrow (q \land r)$. The correct precedence is $\neg > \land > \lor > \rightarrow > \leftrightarrow$. You will lose **2 marks** for this.
> 
> 2. **Confusing converse with contrapositive:** "If $p$ then $q$" has contrapositive "If $\neg q$ then $\neg p$", NOT "If $q$ then $p$" (that is the converse). Examiners explicitly test this.
> 
> 3. **Calling $p \rightarrow q$ a tautology:** It is NOT. A single conditional is a contingency. Only $(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$ is a tautology.
> 
> 4. **Incomplete truth tables:** You must show **all intermediate columns** with arrows explaining the derivation. Skipping intermediate columns costs **1–2 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Mis-application of De Morgan's Law:** When negating a compound statement, flip the connective AND negate each term. $\neg(\forall x \in S, P(x)) \equiv \exists x \in S, \neg P(x)$ (when you reach predicate logic in Module 2).
> 
> 6. **Treating "or" as exclusive OR:** In KTU math, $\lor$ is **inclusive** unless stated otherwise. $p \lor q$ is TRUE when both $p$ and $q$ are true.
> 
> 7. **Algebraic proof "jumps":** Each step in a logical equivalence chain must be justified by citing a specific law. If you skip from $p \lor (q \land r)$ to $(p \lor q) \land (p \lor r)$ without showing the application of the Distributive Law, you lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- **Definitions (must be memorized verbatim):**
  - Proposition: a declarative sentence that is either TRUE or FALSE, never both.
  - Tautology ($\top$): always TRUE under all valuations.
  - Contradiction ($\bot$): always FALSE under all valuations.
  - Contingency: mixed truth values.
  - Logical Equivalence: $P \equiv Q$ iff $P \leftrightarrow Q$ is a tautology.
  - Duality: swap $\land \leftrightarrow \lor$ and $T \leftrightarrow F$.

- **The 5 Connectives** (with precedence $\neg > \land > \lor > \rightarrow > \leftrightarrow$):
  - $\neg p$ — Negation
  - $p \land q$ — Conjunction
  - $p \lor q$ — Disjunction (inclusive)
  - $p \rightarrow q$ — Implication
  - $p \leftrightarrow q$ — Biconditional

- **The 4 Conditional Variants (high-yield):**
  - Conditional: $p \rightarrow q$
  - Converse: $q \rightarrow p$ (NOT equivalent)
  - Inverse: $\neg p \rightarrow \neg q$ (NOT equivalent)
  - Contrapositive: $\neg q \rightarrow \neg p$ (EQUIVALENT to conditional)

- **The 3 Critical Equivalences (must be memorized):**
  - $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q$
  - $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$
  - $\neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \land \neg q$

- **The 2 De Morgan's Laws:**
  - $\neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg p \lor \neg q$
  - $\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q$

- **For a truth table with $n$ variables:** always produce exactly $2^n$ rows. Use systematic binary ordering (T,T), (T,F), (F,T), (F,F).

- **For algebraic proof:** each step must cite the law used. Always state the law's name or number.

- **Implication truth table memory trick:** "$p \rightarrow q$ is false ONLY when $p$ is True and $q$ is False." All other 3 cases are True.

- **For English-to-logic translation:** identify atomic propositions FIRST, then determine the connective structure, then apply the formal connectives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES (INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Truth Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — TRUTH TABLES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Truth Table**
> A **truth table** is a mathematical tabular representation that enumerates every possible combination of truth values (T for true, F for false) for the propositional variables in a logical statement, and systematically computes the resulting truth value of the entire compound proposition under each combination.

A truth table provides a **deterministic, exhaustive, and unambiguous** method to evaluate the validity of any compound proposition constructed from atomic propositions and logical connectives. For $n$ atomic propositions, a truth table contains exactly $2^n$ rows, covering the entire logical sample space.

### 1.2 Atomic vs Compound Propositions

| Concept | Formal Notation | Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomic Proposition** | $p, q, r, \ldots$ | A single, indivisible declarative statement (e.g., "It is raining") |
| **Compound Proposition** | $p \land q$, $p \rightarrow r$ | A statement formed by joining atomic propositions using connectives |
| **Truth Value** | $T$ or $F$ (sometimes $1$ or $0$) | The factual correctness assigned to a proposition |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Light Switch Panel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition: Truth Tables as an Electrical Switch Control Panel**
> Imagine a wall panel with **$n$ light switches** (each switch represents one propositional variable; ON = T, OFF = F). A truth table is the **manufacturer's manual** that lists *every* possible combination of switch positions and tells you, for each combination, exactly whether the final bulb (the compound proposition) will glow or not. The manual never skips a switch combination — it lists all $2^n$ possibilities exhaustively. This is precisely the role a truth table plays in discrete mathematics and digital logic design.

### 1.4 Standard Logical Connectives in KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Symbol | Name | Pronounced As | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\neg p$ | Negation | "NOT $p$" | Unary |
| $p \land q$ | Conjunction | "$p$ AND $q$" | Binary |
| $p \lor q$ | Disjunction | "$p$ OR $q$" | Binary |
| $p \rightarrow q$ | Implication (Conditional) | "$p$ IMPLIES $q$" | Binary |
| $p \leftrightarrow q$ | Biconditional | "$p$ IF AND ONLY IF $q$" | Binary |
| $p \oplus q$ | Exclusive OR (XOR) | "$p$ XOR $q$" | Binary |
| $p \uparrow q$ | NAND (Sheffer Stroke) | "NOT ($p$ AND $q$)" | Binary |
| $p \downarrow q$ | NOR (Peirce Arrow) | "NOT ($p$ OR $q$)" | Binary |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Composition of a Truth Table

A canonical KTU-style truth table is built in the following **column-order convention**:

1. **Leftmost block:** All propositional variables ($p, q, r, \ldots$) listed in alphabetical order.
2. **Middle block:** Sub-expressions of the compound proposition, written in **order of increasing logical complexity** — negations first, then conjunctions/disjunctions, then implications, and finally the main connective.
3. **Rightmost column:** The final compound proposition under evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order?** Writing sub-expressions in increasing complexity dramatically reduces valuation errors and aligns with the KTU board examiner's preferred working style.

### 2.2 Operator Precedence (Descending Order)

$$\neg \;\;>\;\; \land \;\;>\;\; \lor \;\;>\;\; \rightarrow \;\;>\;\; \leftrightarrow$$

Higher precedence means the operator binds **more tightly** (i.e., it is evaluated first). Always use parentheses when in doubt.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Proposition Form | True When ... | False When ... | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\neg p$ | $p$ is F | $p$ is T | Inverter (NOT gate) in digital circuits |
| 2 | $p \land q$ | **Both** $p$ and $q$ are T | **At least one** is F | Series circuit / AND gate |
| 3 | $p \lor q$ | **At least one** of $p, q$ is T | **Both** are F | Parallel circuit / OR gate |
| 4 | $p \rightarrow q$ | $p$ is F **OR** $q$ is T | $p$ is T **AND** $q$ is F | Conditional logic, safety interlocks |
| 5 | $p \leftrightarrow q$ | $p$ and $q$ have **same** truth value | $p$ and $q$ differ | Equivalence checker / XNOR gate |
| 6 | $p \oplus q$ (XOR) | $p$ and $q$ have **different** truth values | $p$ and $q$ are same | Parity bit, half-adder |
| 7 | $p \uparrow q$ (NAND) | **At least one** of $p, q$ is F | **Both** are T | Universally complete gate |
| 8 | $p \downarrow q$ (NOR) | **Both** $p$ and $q$ are F | **At least one** is T | Universally complete gate |

### 2.4 Number of Rows Rule

For a compound proposition containing $n$ distinct propositional variables:

$$\text{Number of distinct rows} \;=\; 2^{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** If two rows in the final column are missing, the table is incomplete. Examiners award **zero marks** for an under-populated truth table. Always verify $2^n$ rows before submission.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Logic Design:** Truth tables are the foundational specification for designing logic gates, multiplexers, adders, and entire CPUs.
- **Boolean Algebra & Switching Theory:** Underpins the minimization of logic circuits (Karnaugh maps, Quine–McCluskey algorithm).
- **Software Verification & Testing:** Exhaustive truth-table evaluation validates conditional logic in safety-critical software (avionics, medical devices).
- **Database Query Optimization:** SQL `WHERE` clauses are evaluated using Boolean logic derived from truth tables.
- **Artificial Intelligence:** Inference engines in propositional logic use truth tables to test entailment and consistency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Truth Tables for All Standard Connectives

#### 3.1.1 Negation ($\neg p$) — Unary Operator

| $p$ | $\neg p$ |
|---|---|
| T | F |
| F | T |

> **Logic:** $\neg p$ simply inverts the truth value of $p$. The negation of T is F, and the negation of F is T. There are $2^{1} = 2$ rows.

---

#### 3.1.2 Conjunction ($p \land q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \land q$ |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | F |
| F | T | F |
| F | F | F |

> **Logic:** The conjunction is true **only** when **both** $p$ and $q$ are true simultaneously. In all other combinations, the conjunction evaluates to false. Total rows = $2^{2} = 4$.

---

#### 3.1.3 Disjunction ($p \lor q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \lor q$ |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | T |
| F | T | T |
| F | F | F |

> **Logic:** The disjunction is true whenever **at least one** of $p$ or $q$ is true. It is false only when both are false. This is the **inclusive OR**.

---

#### 3.1.4 Implication ($p \rightarrow q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | F |
| F | T | T |
| F | F | T |

> **Logic:** The implication is false **only** when the antecedent $p$ is true and the consequent $q$ is false. In all other cases (including when $p$ is false, regardless of $q$), the implication is true. This often surprises students but is a cornerstone of mathematical proof (vacuous truth).

---

#### 3.1.5 Biconditional ($p \leftrightarrow q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \leftrightarrow q$ |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | T |
| T | F | F |
| F | T | F |
| F | F | T |

> **Logic:** The biconditional is true exactly when $p$ and $q$ have **identical** truth values. It can be expressed as $(p \rightarrow q) \land (q \rightarrow p)$.

---

#### 3.1.6 Exclusive OR — XOR ($p \oplus q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \oplus q$ |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | F |
| T | F | T |
| F | T | T |
| F | F | F |

> **Logic:** The XOR is true exactly when $p$ and $q$ have **different** truth values. It is the logical opposite of the biconditional.

---

#### 3.1.7 NAND ($p \uparrow q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \uparrow q$ |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | F |
| T | F | T |
| F | T | T |
| F | F | T |

> **Logic:** NAND is the negation of conjunction. It is true except when both $p$ and $q$ are true. It is **functionally complete** — any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates.

---

#### 3.1.8 NOR ($p \downarrow q$)

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \downarrow q$ |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | F |
| T | F | F |
| F | T | F |
| F | F | T |

> **Logic:** NOR is the negation of disjunction. It is true only when both $p$ and $q$ are false. Like NAND, it is **functionally complete**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Constructing a Truth Table for a Compound Proposition

**Problem (KTU Typical):** Construct the truth table for the compound proposition:

$$(p \rightarrow q) \land (\neg q \rightarrow \neg p)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the number of variables.**
The atomic propositions are $p$ and $q$, so $n = 2$. The table will have $2^{2} = 4$ rows.

**Step 2 — List all $2^n$ input combinations in standard order.**

| $p$ | $q$ |
|---|---|
| T | T |
| T | F |
| F | T |
| F | F |

> **Convention:** KTU examiners expect the first variable to alternate in blocks of $2^{n-1}$, the second in blocks of $2^{n-2}$, and so on. For two variables: T T, T F, F T, F F.

**Step 3 — Add intermediate sub-expression columns in increasing complexity.**

We need: (i) $p \rightarrow q$, (ii) $\neg q$, (iii) $\neg p$, (iv) $\neg q \rightarrow \neg p$, and finally (v) the conjunction of (i) and (iv).

**Step 4 — Evaluate row by row.**

| $p$ | $q$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $\neg q \rightarrow \neg p$ | $(p \rightarrow q) \land (\neg q \rightarrow \neg p)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | F | F | T | T | **T** |
| T | F | F | T | F | F | **F** |
| F | T | T | F | T | T | **T** |
| F | F | T | T | T | T | **T** |

**Step 5 — Verification of each non-trivial cell:**

$$\text{Row 1: } (T \rightarrow T) \land (F \rightarrow F) = T \land T = T$$

$$\text{Row 2: } (T \rightarrow F) \land (T \rightarrow F) = F \land F = F$$

$$\text{Row 3: } (F \rightarrow T) \land (F \rightarrow T) = T \land T = T$$

$$\text{Row 4: } (F \rightarrow F) \land (T \rightarrow T) = T \land T = T$$

**Step 6 — Classification of the Final Column.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Since the final column is NOT all T (Row 2 is F), the statement is NOT a tautology.} \\
\text{Since the final column is NOT all F, the statement is NOT a contradiction.} \\
\text{Hence, the statement is a CONTINGENCY.}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tautology, Contradiction, Contingency

| Final Column Pattern | Classification | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| All entries are **T** | **Tautology** ($\models P$) | Always true, irrespective of inputs | $p \lor \neg p$ |
| All entries are **F** | **Contradiction** ($\models \neg P$) | Always false, irrespective of inputs | $p \land \neg p$ |
| Mixed T and F entries | **Contingency** (Satisfiable) | True for *some* input combinations | $p \rightarrow q$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Whenever a question says "Verify whether the given statement is a tautology/contradiction," the only accepted method is to **build the full truth table** and then **explicitly state the pattern** observed in the final column. Verbal reasoning alone earns **partial credit only**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Tautology Verification

**Problem:** Show that $(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$ is a tautology.

**Step 1:** Two variables ⇒ $2^{2} = 4$ rows.

| $p$ | $q$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg p \lor q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \lor q)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | F | T | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | F | T |
| F | T | T | T | T | T |
| F | F | T | T | T | T |

**Step 2:** Inspect the final column — all four entries are **T**.

$$\therefore \; (p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \lor q) \text{ is a TAUTOLOGY.} \; \blacksquare$$

> **Interpretation:** This proves the fundamental identity $p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q$, one of the most important equivalences in KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Logical Equivalence via Truth Table

**Problem:** Prove that $\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q$ (De Morgan's Law).

| $p$ | $q$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg q$ | $p \lor q$ | $\neg(p \lor q)$ | $\neg p \land \neg q$ | $\neg(p \lor q) \leftrightarrow \neg p \land \neg q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | F | F | T | F | F | T |
| T | F | F | T | T | F | F | T |
| F | T | T | F | T | F | F | T |
| F | F | T | T | F | T | T | T |

Since both columns of $\neg(p \lor q)$ and $\neg p \land \neg q$ produce **identical** patterns (F, F, F, T), the two statements are **logically equivalent**:

$$\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q \;\; \blacksquare$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Truth Table Construction

The following flowchart captures the systematic procedure a student must follow when constructing a truth table for any compound proposition in the KTU 2024 examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Read Compound Proposition P]) --> B[Identify all atomic variables p q r ...]
    B --> C[Compute n equals number of variables]
    C --> D[Number of rows equals 2 raised to n]
    D --> E[List all 2 to the n input combinations in canonical order]
    E --> F[Decompose P into sub expressions]
    F --> G[Order sub expressions by increasing complexity]
    G --> H[Negations and atomic columns first]
    H --> I[Conjunctions and disjunctions next]
    I --> J[Implications and biconditionals later]
    J --> K[Main connective last as final column]
    K --> L[Evaluate every cell row by row]
    L --> M[Double check all sub expression columns]
    M --> N{Inspect final column pattern}
    N -->|All True| O[Conclusion: P is a TAUTOLOGY]
    N -->|All False| P[Conclusion: P is a CONTRADICTION]
    N -->|Mixed True False| Q[Conclusion: P is a CONTINGENCY]
    O --> R([END: Write final classification and justification])
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Logical Connective Selection

This decision tree guides the identification of the correct connective when translating an English sentence into symbolic logic (a frequent KTU question type).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read the English Statement] --> B{Does it contain NOT or It is false that}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use NEGATION symbol NOT]
    B -->|No| D{Does it contain AND or Both}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use CONJUNCTION symbol AND]
    D -->|No| F{Does it contain OR or Either}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use DISJUNCTION symbol OR]
    F -->|No| H{Does it contain If then or Implies}
    H -->|Yes| I[Use IMPLICATION symbol right arrow]
    H -->|No| J{Does it contain If and only if or Equivalent to}
    J -->|Yes| K[Use BICONDITIONAL symbol left right arrow]
    J -->|No| L[Statement is atomic no connective needed]
    C --> M([Translate to symbolic form])
    E --> M
    G --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.3 Truth Table Expansion Schema (3-Variable Cascade)

The diagram below illustrates how the standard row-generation pattern cascades as the number of variables increases from 1 to 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N1["n = 1: 2 to the 1 = 2 rows"]
        A1["T"]
        A2["F"]
    end
    subgraph N2["n = 2: 2 to the 2 = 4 rows"]
        B1["T T"]
        B2["T F"]
        B3["F T"]
        B4["F F"]
    end
    subgraph N3["n = 3: 2 to the 3 = 8 rows"]
        C1["T T T"]
        C2["T T F"]
        C3["T F T"]
        C4["T F F"]
        C5["F T T"]
        C6["F T F"]
        C7["F F T"]
        C8["F F F"]
    end
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that the leftmost variable changes slowest (every 4 rows for $n=3$), the middle variable changes every 2 rows, and the rightmost variable changes every row. This is the **canonical KTU pattern** that examiners expect for binary count-up ordering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a truth table. How many rows are required for a compound proposition with 3 propositional variables?**

**Model Answer:**

A truth table is a tabular method that lists all possible truth-value combinations of the propositional variables appearing in a compound proposition and determines the resulting truth value of the compound statement for each combination.

For $n = 3$ propositional variables, the required number of rows is:

$$\text{Number of rows} = 2^{n} = 2^{3} = 8$$

These 8 rows exhaustively cover every combination of T/F assignments to $p, q, r$, ensuring no logical case is omitted during the analysis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between a tautology and a contradiction? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **tautology** is a compound proposition whose truth value is **true for every possible assignment** of truth values to its propositional variables.

$$\text{Example: } \; p \lor \neg p \;\; \text{(Law of Excluded Middle)}$$

A **contradiction** is a compound proposition whose truth value is **false for every possible assignment** of truth values to its propositional variables.

$$\text{Example: } \; p \land \neg p \;\; \text{(Law of Non-Contradiction)}$$

A proposition that is neither a tautology nor a contradiction is called a **contingency**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**a)** Construct the truth table for the compound proposition $p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow p)$. Hence classify it as a tautology, contradiction, or contingency. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Using truth tables, verify the logical equivalence $\neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg p \lor \neg q$ (De Morgan's Law). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Variables are $p, q$, so $n = 2 \Rightarrow 2^{2} = 4$ rows.

**Step 2:** Build the table, evaluating the inner implication $q \rightarrow p$ first, then the outer implication $p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow p)$.

| $p$ | $q$ | $q \rightarrow p$ | $p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow p)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | T | T |
| T | F | T | T |
| F | T | F | T |
| F | F | T | T |

**Step 3 — Cell-wise verification:**

$$\text{Row 1: } T \rightarrow (T \rightarrow T) = T \rightarrow T = T$$

$$\text{Row 2: } T \rightarrow (F \rightarrow T) = T \rightarrow T = T$$

$$\text{Row 3: } F \rightarrow (T \rightarrow F) = F \rightarrow F = T$$

$$\text{Row 4: } F \rightarrow (F \rightarrow F) = F \rightarrow T = T$$

**Step 4 — Classification:**
Since the final column contains **only T values**, the proposition $p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow p)$ is a **TAUTOLOGY**. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** We must show both sides have identical truth values for all $2^{2} = 4$ input combinations.

| $p$ | $q$ | $\neg p$ | $\neg q$ | $p \land q$ | $\neg(p \land q)$ | $\neg p \lor \neg q$ | LHS $\leftrightarrow$ RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | F | F | T | F | F | T |
| T | F | F | T | F | T | T | T |
| F | T | T | F | F | T | T | T |
| F | F | T | T | F | T | T | T |

**Step 2 — Observation:**
The columns for $\neg(p \land q)$ and $\neg p \lor \neg q$ both produce the pattern $(F, T, T, T)$. Since they are identical for every row, the two statements are **logically equivalent**.

$$\therefore \; \neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg p \lor \neg q \;\; \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**a)** Construct the truth table for the proposition $(p \land q) \rightarrow (p \lor q)$. State whether it is a tautology, contradiction, or contingency. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write the truth table for $p \oplus q$ (exclusive OR). Hence, show that $p \oplus q \equiv (p \lor q) \land \neg(p \land q)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Variables: $p, q \Rightarrow 2^{2} = 4$ rows.

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \land q$ | $p \lor q$ | $(p \land q) \rightarrow (p \lor q)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | T | T | T |
| T | F | F | T | T |
| F | T | F | T | T |
| F | F | F | F | T |

**Step 2 — Cell verification for the critical Row 3:**

$$\text{Row 3: } (F \land T) \rightarrow (F \lor T) = F \rightarrow T = T$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**
Final column is **all T** ⇒ the proposition is a **TAUTOLOGY**.

**Intuition:** Whenever $p \land q$ holds true, $p \lor q$ must also hold true (since conjunction is a strictly stronger condition than disjunction). Hence the implication is never violated. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Truth table for $p \oplus q$:**

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \oplus q$ |
|---|---|---|
| T | T | F |
| T | F | T |
| F | T | T |
| F | F | F |

**Step 2 — Truth table for the expanded form $(p \lor q) \land \neg(p \land q)$:**

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \lor q$ | $p \land q$ | $\neg(p \land q)$ | $(p \lor q) \land \neg(p \land q)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T | T | T | T | F | F |
| T | F | T | F | T | T |
| F | T | T | F | T | T |
| F | F | F | F | T | F |

**Step 3 — Comparison:**
The two rightmost columns are **identical**: $(F, T, T, F)$. Therefore, by the truth table method of logical equivalence:

$$\therefore \; p \oplus q \equiv (p \lor q) \land \neg(p \land q) \;\; \blacksquare$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Missing rows:** Always verify the row count equals $2^n$. A 3-variable table with only 4 rows = **automatic 50% mark deduction**.
> 2. **Implication error:** Many students incorrectly mark $F \rightarrow T$ as F. **Remember:** An implication is false **only** when antecedent is T and consequent is F. In all other 3 cases it is T.
> 3. **Operator precedence:** Forgetting parentheses around $p \land q$ in $\neg(p \land q)$ reverses the meaning. Always parenthesize before negating.
> 4. **Biconditional confusion:** Students often confuse $\leftrightarrow$ (biconditional) with $\rightarrow$ (implication). Biconditional is T only when both sides have the **same** truth value.
> 5. **No classification statement:** Even if the table is correct, failing to explicitly write "Tautology / Contradiction / Contingency" at the end forfeits the conclusion marks (typically 1 mark).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Truth Tables (Module 1)**

- ☐ A **truth table** is an exhaustive tabular evaluation of a compound proposition over **all** $2^n$ input combinations.
- ☐ **Number of rows** for $n$ propositional variables is **$2^{n}$** — never skip any.
- ☐ Standard **canonical ordering** of input rows: binary count-up (TT, TF, FT, FF for $n=2$).
- ☐ **Negation** $\neg p$ inverts T to F and F to T.
- ☐ **Conjunction** $p \land q$ is T **only** when both are T; otherwise F.
- ☐ **Disjunction** $p \lor q$ is F **only** when both are F; otherwise T (inclusive OR).
- ☐ **Implication** $p \rightarrow q$ is F **only** when $p = T$ and $q = F$; T in all other 3 cases.
- ☐ **Biconditional** $p \leftrightarrow q$ is T when $p$ and $q$ have **identical** truth values.
- ☐ **XOR** $p \oplus q$ is T when $p$ and $q$ have **different** truth values.
- ☐ **NAND** $p \uparrow q$ is F **only** when both are T; **functionally complete**.
- ☐ **NOR** $p \downarrow q$ is T **only** when both are F; **functionally complete**.
- ☐ **Tautology** = final column all T; **Contradiction** = final column all F; **Contingency** = mixed pattern.
- ☐ To prove two statements are **logically equivalent**, the **biconditional column** must be **all T**, OR their individual truth columns must be **identical**.
- ☐ **Operator precedence** (descending): $\neg, \land, \lor, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ — always parenthesize for safety.
- ☐ A truth table with **missing rows, missing classification, or wrong implication rule** will attract heavy mark deductions under KTU 2024 valuation norms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditional Propositions and Logical Equivalence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES (INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Conditional Propositions and Logical Equivalence # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Conditional Propositions (Material Implication) A **conditional proposition** (also called a **material implication** or **implication**) is a compound proposition of the form $p \rightarrow q$ (read as "if $p$ then $q$"), where: - $p$ is called the **hypothesis** (or **antecedent**) - $q$ is called the **conclusion** (or **consequent**) $$p \rightarrow q \;\;\text{is defined as}\;\; \sim p \vee q$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A conditional statement $p \rightarrow q$ is **false only when $p$ is true and $q$ is false**. In every other combination, the conditional is **true**. This is the most counter-intuitive part for students — a false hypothesis always makes the conditional true, regardless of the conclusion. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **promotional coupon** that says: *"If you spend more than ₹1000, then you get a free gift."* Let: - $p$ = "You spend more than ₹1000" - $q$ = "You get a free gift" Now consider the four scenarios: 1. You spend ₹1500 AND you get the gift → **Promise kept** → TRUE 2. You spend ₹1500 BUT no gift → **Promise broken** → FALSE 3. You spend only ₹500 AND you get a gift → **Promise not triggered** → TRUE (no obligation existed) 4. You spend only ₹500 AND no gift → **Promise not triggered** → TRUE This everyday scenario perfectly mirrors the truth-functional behavior of $p \rightarrow q$. > [!NOTE] > In everyday English, "if...then" often suggests a **causal relationship**, but in propositional logic, $p \rightarrow q$ is a **truth-functional operator** — it depends only on truth values, not on cause-and-effect. --- ## 1.2 Related Conditional Forms Given $p \rightarrow q$, three related statements are mathematically important: | Form | Symbolic Form | English Reading | |------|---------------|-----------------| | Converse | $q \rightarrow p$ | If $q$ then $p$ | | Inverse | $\sim p \rightarrow \sim q$ | If not $p$ then not $q$ | | Contrapositive | $\sim q \rightarrow \sim p$ | If not $q$ then not $p$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A conditional statement is **logically equivalent to its contrapositive** ($p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim q \rightarrow \sim p$). However, it is **NOT equivalent** to its converse or inverse. The converse and inverse are logically equivalent **to each other** (but not to the original conditional). --- ## 1.3 Biconditional Proposition A **biconditional proposition** $p \leftrightarrow q$ is read as "$p$ **if and only if** $q$" (abbreviated "p iff q"). $$p \leftrightarrow q \;\equiv\; (p \rightarrow q) \wedge (q \rightarrow p) \;\equiv\; (p \wedge q) \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q)$$ The biconditional is **true precisely when $p$ and $q$ have the same truth value** (both true or both false). > [!NOTE] > Biconditional is essentially a **logical equality test** between two propositions — they are either equivalent in truth or not. --- ## 1.4 Logical Equivalence Two propositions $A$ and $B$ are **logically equivalent** (written $A \equiv B$) if and only if they have **identical truth values** for every possible assignment of truth values to their component propositions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** $A \equiv B$ if and only if $A \leftrightarrow B$ is a **tautology**. Equivalently, the last column of the truth table for $A$ and $B$ must be **identical**. ### 1.4.1 Special Propositional Forms - **Tautology (T):** A compound proposition that is **always true**, regardless of the truth values of its components. - **Contradiction (F):** A compound proposition that is **always false**. - **Contingency:** A compound proposition that is **neither a tautology nor a contradiction** — its truth value depends on the input. --- ## 1.5 Intuitive Summary with Analogy Think of logic as a **universal language translator**: - **Conditional** is like a one-way arrow → from hypothesis to conclusion. - **Biconditional** is like a two-way bridge ↔ where both sides must match. - **Logical Equivalence** is like finding two recipes that always produce the same dish, no matter what ingredients you choose. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Truth Table of Conditional $p \rightarrow q$ The truth table of $p \rightarrow q$ is the **single most important table** in this module for KTU exams. | $p$ | $q$ | $\sim p$ | $\sim p \vee q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | |-----|-----|----------|----------------|-------------------| | T | T | F | T | **T** | | T | F | F | F | **F** | | F | T | T | T | **T** | | F | F | T | T | **T** | > [!NOTE] > The last two columns ($\sim p \vee q$ and $p \rightarrow q$) are **identical**, confirming the defining equivalence $p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim p \vee q$. --- ## 2.2 Truth Table of Biconditional $p \leftrightarrow q$ | $p$ | $q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $q \rightarrow p$ | $p \leftrightarrow q$ | |-----|-----|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------| | T | T | T | T | **T** | | T | F | F | T | **F** | | F | T | T | F | **F** | | F | F | T | T | **T** | --- ## 2.3 Why Is $F \rightarrow T$ True? (Conceptual Deep Dive) This is the **most counter-intuitive row** in the table and a frequent KTU conceptual question. Reasons to remember: 1. **Logical (truth-functional) definition:** $p \rightarrow q$ is a *promise* about the future, true unless explicitly broken. 2. **Algebraic verification:** $p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim p \vee q$. If $p$ is F, then $\sim p$ is T, so $\sim p \vee q$ is T. 3. **Counter-example with quantification:** $\forall x \in \emptyset,\; P(x)$ is vacuously true — similar to the empty-promise case. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following equivalence table is the heart of Module 1. Memorize it thoroughly for KTU ESE.** ### 2.4.1 Fundamental Logical Equivalences | # | Law | Equivalence | |---|-----|-------------| | 1 | Identity | $p \equiv p \wedge T \equiv p \vee F$ | | 2 | Domination | $p \vee T \equiv T$, $\;\;p \wedge F \equiv F$ | | 3 | Idempotent | $p \vee p \equiv p$, $\;\;p \wedge p \equiv p$ | | 4 | Double Negation | $\sim(\sim p) \equiv p$ | | 5 | Commutative | $p \vee q \equiv q \vee p$, $\;\;p \wedge q \equiv q \wedge p$ | | 6 | Associative | $(p \vee q) \vee r \equiv p \vee (q \vee r)$ | | 7 | Distributive | $p \wedge (q \vee r) \equiv (p \wedge q) \vee (p \wedge r)$ | | 8 | Distributive | $p \vee (q \wedge r) \equiv (p \vee q) \wedge (p \vee r)$ | | 9 | De Morgan's | $\sim(p \wedge q) \equiv \sim p \vee \sim q$ | | 10 | De Morgan's | $\sim(p \vee q) \equiv \sim p \wedge \sim q$ | | 11 | Absorption | $p \vee (p \wedge q) \equiv p$ | | 12 | Absorption | $p \wedge (p \vee q) \equiv p$ | | 13 | Negation | $p \vee \sim p \equiv T$ (Law of Excluded Middle) | | 14 | Negation | $p \wedge \sim p \equiv F$ (Law of Non-Contradiction) | ### 2.4.2 Conditional & Biconditional Equivalences (KTU High-Yield) | # | Identity | Equivalence | |---|----------|-------------| | C1 | Implication | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim p \vee q$ | | C2 | Converse | $q \rightarrow p$ (NOT equivalent to $p \rightarrow q$) | | C3 | Contrapositive | $\sim q \rightarrow \sim p \equiv p \rightarrow q$ | | C4 | Inverse | $\sim p \rightarrow \sim q$ (NOT equivalent to $p \rightarrow q$) | | C5 | Negation of Implication | $\sim(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \wedge \sim q$ | | C6 | Biconditional | $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \wedge (q \rightarrow p)$ | | C7 | Biconditional (alt) | $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \wedge q) \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q)$ | | C8 | Negation of Biconditional | $\sim(p \leftrightarrow q) \equiv p \oplus q$ (exclusive or) | | C9 | Implication Distributivity | $p \rightarrow (q \wedge r) \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \wedge (p \rightarrow r)$ | | C10 | Implication Distributivity | $p \rightarrow (q \vee r) \equiv (p \rightarrow q) \vee (p \rightarrow r)$ | | C11 | Exportation | $(p \wedge q) \rightarrow r \equiv p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow r)$ | | C12 | Contraposition | $p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim q \rightarrow \sim p$ | > [!WARNING] > In the table above, **C5** and **C8** are derived as follows and **students commonly lose marks** for not knowing the negation of implication: > $$\sim(p \rightarrow q) \equiv \sim(\sim p \vee q) \equiv p \wedge \sim q$$ --- ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Digital Circuit Design:** Conditional logic is implemented using **IMPLY gates** and forms the basis of multiplexers. - **Programming:** Short-circuit evaluation in `if` statements mirrors $p \rightarrow q$ (only when $p$ is true is $q$ evaluated). - **AI/Expert Systems:** Rule-based inference engines (forward chaining) use $p \rightarrow q$ rules. - **Database Query Optimization:** Boolean query rewrites rely on logical equivalences. - **Formal Verification & Software Testing:** Pre-conditions and post-conditions are formalized using implications. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the most exam-critical equivalences. No steps are skipped. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1: $p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim p \vee q$ (Using Truth Table) **Step 1:** Construct a truth table with $2^2 = 4$ rows for the atomic propositions $p$ and $q$. | $p$ | $q$ | $\sim p$ | $\sim p \vee q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | |-----|-----|----------|----------------|-------------------| | T | T | F | F $\vee$ T = **T** | **T** | | T | F | F | F $\vee$ F = **F** | **F** | | F | T | T | T $\vee$ T = **T** | **T** | | F | F | T | T $\vee$ F = **T** | **T** | **Step 2:** Compare the last two columns. **Conversion Logic:** The column for $\sim p \vee q$ produces T, F, T, T. The column for $p \rightarrow q$ also produces T, F, T, T. Since both columns are **identical for every assignment**, the propositions are logically equivalent. $$\boxed{\therefore p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim p \vee q}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: $\sim(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \wedge \sim q$ (Algebraic Method) We use the defining equivalence C1 and De Morgan's law (Law 9). **Step 1:** Replace $p \rightarrow q$ using C1. $$\sim(p \rightarrow q) \equiv \sim(\sim p \vee q)$$ **Conversion Logic:** By Law C1, $p \rightarrow q$ is the same as $\sim p \vee q$, so negating the LHS is the same as negating $\sim p \vee q$. **Step 2:** Apply De Morgan's Law (Law 9) to break the negation over the disjunction. $$\sim(\sim p \vee q) \equiv \sim(\sim p) \wedge \sim q$$ **Conversion Logic:** De Morgan's law states that negating a disjunction gives the conjunction of the negations. **Step 3:** Apply the Double Negation Law (Law 4) to simplify $\sim(\sim p)$. $$\sim(\sim p) \wedge \sim q \equiv p \wedge \sim q$$ **Conversion Logic:** $\sim(\sim p)$ is the negation of negation, which is just $p$ itself. **Step 4:** Final result. $$\boxed{\therefore \sim(p \rightarrow q) \equiv p \wedge \sim q}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: $p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \wedge q) \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q)$ (Using Truth Table) **Step 1:** Build the full truth table. | $p$ | $q$ | $p \wedge q$ | $\sim p$ | $\sim q$ | $\sim p \wedge \sim q$ | $(p \wedge q) \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q)$ | $p \leftrightarrow q$ | |-----|-----|--------------|----------|----------|------------------------|-------------------------------------------|----------------------| | T | T | T | F | F | F | T $\vee$ F = **T** | **T** | | T | F | F | F | T | F | F $\vee$ F = **F** | **F** | | F | T | F | T | F | F | F $\vee$ F = **F** | **F** | | F | F | F | T | T | T | F $\vee$ T = **T** | **T** | **Step 2:** Compare the last two columns. They are **identical** (T, F, F, T). **Conversion Logic:** Both expressions evaluate to true exactly when $p$ and $q$ have the same truth value. $$\boxed{\therefore p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \wedge q) \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Contrapositive Equivalence $p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim q \rightarrow \sim p$ **Step 1:** Apply C1 to the right-hand side. $$\sim q \rightarrow \sim p \equiv \sim(\sim q) \vee \sim p$$ **Step 2:** Apply Double Negation (Law 4) to $\sim(\sim q)$. $$\sim(\sim q) \vee \sim p \equiv q \vee \sim p$$ **Step 3:** Apply Commutative Law (Law 5) to swap the operands. $$q \vee \sim p \equiv \sim p \vee q$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the right-hand side from C1. $$\sim p \vee q \equiv p \rightarrow q$$ **Step 5:** Therefore. $$\boxed{\therefore p \rightarrow q \equiv \sim q \rightarrow \sim p}$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: $(p \rightarrow q) \wedge (p \rightarrow r) \equiv p \rightarrow (q \wedge r)$ (Truth Table) | $p$ | $q$ | $r$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | $p \rightarrow r$ | $(p\rightarrow q)\wedge(p\rightarrow r)$ | $q \wedge r$ | $p \rightarrow (q \wedge r)$ | |-----|-----|-----|-------------------|-------------------|------------------------------------------|--------------|-----------------------------| | T | T | T | T | T | **T** | T | **T** | | T | T | F | T | F | **F** | F | **F** | | T | F | T | F | T | **F** | F | **F** | | T | F | F | F | F | **F** | F | **F** | | F | T | T | T | T | **T** | T | **T** | | F | T | F | T | T | **T** | F | **T** | | F | F | T | T | T | **T** | F | **T** | | F | F | F | T | T | **T** | F | **T** | **Conversion Logic:** Both columns 6 and 8 are **identical (T, F, F, F, T, T, T, T)**, confirming the equivalence. $$\boxed{\therefore (p \rightarrow q) \wedge (p \rightarrow r) \equiv p \rightarrow (q \wedge r)}$$ --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Proving $\sim(p \vee (\sim p \wedge q)) \equiv \sim p \wedge \sim q$ (Algebraic Workout) This is a typical KTU 14-mark question where students must show stepwise simplification. **Step 1:** Apply De Morgan's Law (Law 10) to break the outer negation. $$\sim(p \vee (\sim p \wedge q)) \equiv \sim p \wedge \sim(\sim p \wedge q)$$ **Step 2:** Apply De Morgan's Law (Law 9) to the inner negation. $$\sim p \wedge \sim(\sim p \wedge q) \equiv \sim p \wedge (\sim(\sim p) \vee \sim q)$$ **Step 3:** Apply Double Negation (Law 4) — $\sim(\sim p) \equiv p$. $$\sim p \wedge (p \vee \sim q)$$ **Step 4:** Apply Distributive Law (Law 7) — distribute $\sim p$ over the disjunction. $$\sim p \wedge (p \vee \sim q) \equiv (\sim p \wedge p) \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q)$$ **Step 5:** Apply Negation Law (Law 14) — $\sim p \wedge p \equiv F$. $$(\sim p \wedge p) \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q) \equiv F \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q)$$ **Step 6:** Apply Identity Law (Law 1) — $F \vee X \equiv X$. $$F \vee (\sim p \wedge \sim q) \equiv \sim p \wedge \sim q$$ **Step 7:** Final result. $$\boxed{\therefore \sim(p \vee (\sim p \wedge q)) \equiv \sim p \wedge \sim q}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question pattern**. The valuation key expects students to mention the **name of each law** used at every step. Forgetting the law name typically costs **0.5 to 1 mark per step**. --- ## 3.7 Master Algorithm: How to Prove Logical Equivalence (KTU Examiner's Checklist) | Step | Action | Marks Weightage | |------|--------|-----------------| | 1 | Identify the LHS and RHS expressions | Conceptual clarity | | 2 | Choose a method: (a) Truth Table, (b) Algebraic Laws | Strategy | | 3 | Show every transformation step clearly | Stepwise deduction | | 4 | Mention the name of the law used at each step | Marks awarded here | | 5 | Conclude with a $\therefore$ symbol and boxed result | Conclusion | | 6 | For truth tables, ensure **all $2^n$ rows** are filled | Completeness | --- ## 3.8 Tautology / Contradiction / Contingency Detection To classify a compound proposition $P$: 1. Build its complete truth table. 2. Observe the final column: - **All T** → Tautology - **All F** → Contradiction - **Mixed T and F** → Contingency > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trick:** If a proposition is equivalent to a single variable $p$, it is a **contingency** (neither). If it reduces to $T$ or $F$, it is a tautology or contradiction, respectively. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics This section provides **Mermaid-based structural and conceptual diagrams** to visually anchor the relationships between conditional, biconditional, and equivalent forms. --- ## 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of Propositions ```mermaid graph TD propRoot["Compound Propositions"] propRoot --> condType["Conditional Type"] propRoot --> bicondType["Biconditional Type"] propRoot --> equivType["Equivalence Relations"] condType --> impl1["Implication p implies q"] condType --> conv1["Converse q implies p"] condType --> inv1["Inverse not p implies not q"] condType --> cnt1["Contrapositive not q implies not p"] bicondType --> iff1["Biconditional p iff q"] iff1 --> def1["Definition 1: p implies q AND q implies p"] iff1 --> def2["Definition 2: p AND q OR not p AND not q"] equivType --> taut1["Tautology - Always True"] equivType --> cont1["Contradiction - Always False"] equivType --> cng1["Contingency - Depends on Input"] impl1 -.->|"EQUIVALENT"| cnt1 conv1 -.->|"EQUIVALENT"| inv1 style propRoot fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style condType fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style bicondType fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff style equivType fill:#a52a2a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style impl1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style conv1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style inv1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style cnt1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style iff1 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000 style def1 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000 style def2 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000 style taut1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style cont1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style cng1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 ``` > **Figure 4.1:** The hierarchical taxonomy of compound propositions, showing the equivalence between **Conditional** and **Contrapositive**, and between **Converse** and **Inverse**. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Logical Implication Evaluation ```mermaid flowchart LR inP["Input p
Atomic Proposition"] inQ["Input q
Atomic Proposition"] negGate["NOT Gate
Generates not p"] orGate["OR Gate
not p OR q"] evalNode["Evaluation Node
Material Implication"] outRes["Output
Truth Value of p implies q"] inP --> negGate negGate --> orGate inQ --> orGate orGate --> evalNode inP --> evalNode evalNode --> outRes inP -->|Truth Value| inP inQ -->|Truth Value| inQ negGate -->|Used to model F implies T case| orGate style inP fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000 style inQ fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000 style negGate fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style orGate fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style evalNode fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000 style outRes fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 ``` > **Figure 4.2:** Block-level functional flow showing how a material implication $p \rightarrow q$ is **computationally constructed** as $\sim p \vee q$. This is precisely how IMPLY gates are built in digital logic using NOT and OR gates. --- ## 4.3 Truth Table Flow for Conditional Statement ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode["START: Truth Table Construction"] n2["2^2 = 4 Rows"] row1["Row 1: p=T, q=T"] row2["Row 2: p=T, q=F"] row3["Row 3: p=F, q=T"] row4["Row 4: p=F, q=F"] e1["Output: T"] e2["Output: F"] e3["Output: T"] e4["Output: T"] classify["Classification"] cTaut["Not a Tautology"] cCrit["Critical FALSE row
p=T and q=F"] startNode --> n2 n2 --> row1 n2 --> row2 n2 --> row3 n2 --> row4 row1 --> e1 row2 --> e2 row3 --> e3 row4 --> e4 e1 --> classify e2 --> classify e3 --> classify e4 --> classify e2 --> cCrit classify --> cTaut style startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style n2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style row1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style row2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style row3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style row4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style e1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000 style e2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style e3 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000 style e4 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000 style classify fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style cTaut fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 style cCrit fill:#ea9999,stroke:#000 ``` > **Figure 4.3:** Visual flow of the truth-table construction process for $p \rightarrow q$, highlighting the **single critical false row** (where $p$ is true and $q$ is false). --- ## 4.4 Relationship Map: Logical Equivalence ↔ Biconditional ↔ Tautology ```mermaid graph LR lhs["Compound Expression A"] rhs["Compound Expression B"] bicond["A iff B
Biconditional Statement"] tautCheck["Check if A iff B
is a Tautology"] resultA["A and B are
Logically Equivalent"] resultB["A and B are NOT
Logically Equivalent"] lhs --> bicond rhs --> bicond bicond --> tautCheck tautCheck -->|"YES All T"| resultA tautCheck -->|"NO Mixed or All F"| resultB style lhs fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000 style rhs fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000 style bicond fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style tautCheck fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style resultA fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000 style resultB fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000 ``` > **Figure 4.4:** Decision-flow showing the **standard KTU method** for verifying logical equivalence using the biconditional-tautology test. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme assessment pattern** (Bloom's Taxonomy Levels: L1–L4). Mark distribution and valuation style match the official KTU Board pattern. --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | CO1 | Bloom Level: L1 (Remember)]** **Define a conditional proposition. When is a conditional statement false?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): A **conditional proposition** is a compound statement of the form $p \rightarrow q$ (read as "if $p$, then $q$"), where $p$ is the **hypothesis** and $q$ is the **conclusion**. A conditional statement $p \rightarrow q$ is **false if and only if $p$ is true and $q$ is false**. In all other cases (TT, FT, FF), the conditional is true. **Truth Table:** | $p$ | $q$ | $p \rightarrow q$ | |-----|-----|-------------------| | T | T | T | | T | F | **F** | | F | T | T | | F | F | T | **(3 Marks: Definition 1.5 + False condition 0.5 + Truth Table 1)** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam -
