### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Universal Generalization (UG) and Existential Generalization (EG) with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **UG** infers $\forall x\, P(x)$ from $P(c)$ where $c$ is **arbitrary** and subject to the restrictions above. (1 mark)
- **EG** infers $\exists x\, P(x)$ from $P(c)$ where $c$ is a **specific, named** element; no arbitrariness is required. (1 mark)
- **Example:** From “An *arbitrary* even number squared is even,” UG yields “All even numbers squared are even.” From “4 is even,” EG yields “Some number is even.” (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State the four rules of inference for quantified statements. (7 marks)
**(b)** Using the Rule of Universal Generalization, prove that the following argument is valid. (7 marks)

**Premises:**
1. $\forall x\,(P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$
2. $\forall x\,(Q(x) \rightarrow R(x))$
3. $\forall x\,(R(x) \rightarrow S(x))$

**Conclusion:** $\forall x\,(P(x) \rightarrow S(x))$

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The Four Rules (7 marks):**

1. **Universal Instantiation (UI):** $\forall x\, P(x) \Rightarrow P(c)$ for any $c$ in the domain. (1¾ marks)
2. **Universal Generalization (UG):** $P(c)$ for arbitrary $c \Rightarrow \forall x\, P(x)$, subject to the four restrictions. (1¾ marks)
3. **Existential Instantiation (EI):** $\exists x\, P(x) \Rightarrow P(c)$ where $c$ is a **fresh** constant. (1¾ marks)
4. **Existential Generalization (EG):** $P(c)$ for a specific $c \Rightarrow \exists x\, P(x)$. (1¾ marks)

**Part (b) — Full Proof (7 marks):**

| Step | Statement | Justification |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P(c) \rightarrow Q(c)$ | UI on Premise 1 with $x = c$ |
| 2 | $Q(c) \rightarrow R(c)$ | UI on Premise 2 with $x = c$ |
| 3 | $R(c) \rightarrow S(c)$ | UI on Premise 3 with $x = c$ |
| 4 | $P(c) \rightarrow R(c)$ | Hypothetical Syllogism on Steps 1 and 2 |
| 5 | $P(c) \rightarrow S(c)$ | Hypothetical Syllogism on Steps 4 and 3 |
| 6 | $\forall x\,(P(x) \rightarrow S(x))$ | UG on Step 5 — $c$ is arbitrary, not in any premise, and there are no open assumptions at this point |

> [!NOTE]
> The proof above uses **only** UI, propositional rules, and one final UG. Each application of UI explicitly says “with $x = c$” so the examiner can see that the same arbitrary constant is reused — a hallmark of clean KTU answers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain with a suitable example why a constant introduced by **Existential Instantiation (EI)** cannot be generalized using **UG**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Using UG, prove the validity of the following argument. (7 marks)

**Premises:**
1. $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$
2. $\forall x\, B(x)$

**Conclusion:** $\forall x\, A(x)$ **is NOT valid** — instead, prove: $\forall x\, B(x)$ implies the existence of a counter-example? *Corrected intended conclusion:* $\forall x\,(B(x) \rightarrow A(x))$ is **not** provable. **Prove the correct conclusion** $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$ is implied by the premises. (Restated below for clarity.)

*Re-statement of (b):* Given $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$, prove $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$ trivially. **A more interesting task:** From the same Premise 1 above and the additional premise $\forall x\, A(x)$, prove $\forall x\, B(x)$ using UG.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Why EI-introduced constants resist UG (7 marks):**

EI takes $\exists x\, P(x)$ and produces $P(c)$ where $c$ is a **specific witness**. (2 marks)

The constant $c$ is bound to a particular element that *happens* to satisfy $P$. It is not arbitrary; it is fixed by the existential claim. (2 marks)

**Example:** From $\exists x\,(\text{Prime}(x) \wedge \text{Even}(x))$, EI gives $P(2)$, namely “2 is prime and 2 is even.” Applying UG to conclude $\forall x\,(\text{Prime}(x) \wedge \text{Even}(x))$ would falsely claim every number is even and prime. Hence UG is invalid here. (2 marks)

**Conclusion (1 mark):** UG is a *generalizing* rule, while EI produces a *specific* witness. The two rules operate on different kinds of constants, and the UG restriction forbids generalizing an EI-introduced constant.

**Part (b) — Proof using UG (7 marks):**

| Step | Statement | Justification |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Let $c$ be an arbitrary constant | Setup for UG |
| 2 | $A(c) \rightarrow B(c)$ | UI on $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$ with $x = c$ |
| 3 | $A(c)$ | Assumption, begin Conditional Proof |
| 4 | $B(c)$ | MP on Steps 2 and 3 |
| 5 | $A(c) \rightarrow B(c)$ | CP, discharging the assumption of Step 3 |
| 6 | $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(c) \rightarrow B(x))$ | **Renaming the constant to variable** to prepare for UG |
| 7 | $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$ | UG on Step 6 — $c$ is arbitrary, $c$ is not in any premise, and no open assumptions remain |

**Verification of UG restrictions at Step 7:** (built into the model answer)
- $c$ is arbitrary ✓
- $c$ is absent from the premise $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$ ✓
- $c$ does not appear in any open assumption (Step 3 was discharged at Step 5) ✓
- The premise does not single out $c$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on UG questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to declare $c$ arbitrary.** Writing $P(a)$ from a UI step and then immediately concluding $\forall x\, P(x)$ without *first* stating “Let $c$ be an arbitrary element” is a **2–3 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Using UG inside an open CP/PC scope.** The constant $c$ must not appear in any undischarged assumption. Closing the scope (e.g., by CP) **before** applying UG is essential; applying it mid-scope is a **logical error worth full-step deduction**.
> 3. **Confusing UG with EG.** UG requires an *arbitrary* $c$; EG accepts a *specific* $c$. Mixing them up leads to invalid proofs and is heavily penalized.
> 4. **Omitting the justification column.** KTU examiners award ½ to 1 mark per justified line. A bare list of formulas with no “UI, Premise 1, $x = c$” tag loses easy marks.
> 5. **Renaming the bound variable incorrectly.** Writing $\forall c\,(A(c) \rightarrow B(c))$ instead of $\forall x\,(A(x) \rightarrow B(x))$ is technically harmless but cosmetically penalized; use a fresh variable $x$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **UG in one line:** From $P(c)$ for **arbitrary** $c$, infer $\forall x\, P(x)$.
- **The four UG restrictions (memorize verbatim):**
  1. $c$ is arbitrary.
  2. $c \notin$ any premise.
  3. $c \notin$ any open assumption.
  4. No premise in the derivation of $P(c)$ mentions $c$ specifically.
- **UG is a “closing” move.** It is almost always the **last** step of a proof; it generalizes what has just been shown.
- **Pair UG with UI.** UI specializes a universal claim; UG generalizes a result. Together they let you move freely between the global and the local in a proof.
- **EG is the safer existential companion.** While UG is restrictive, EG has no restrictions — any specific witness $P(c)$ lets you claim $\exists x\, P(x)$.
- **EI and UG do not mix.** A constant introduced by EI is *named*, not *arbitrary*, and therefore cannot be generalized by UG.
- **CP + UG is the standard combo.** Open a CP scope, prove $P(c) \rightarrow Q(c)$, close the scope, then apply UG.
- **Cite the rule every time.** In a 14-mark answer, every UI, MP, CP, and UG must carry a one-line justification.
- **Sanity-check with truth tables or Venn diagrams** when the domain is small. If the argument is invalid, no amount of UG can rescue it.
- **Common KTU 3-marker:** “State UG and its restrictions.” Memorize the rule statement and the four restrictions as a single paragraph.
- **Common KTU 7-marker:** Prove $\forall x\,(P(x) \rightarrow R(x))$ from $\forall x\,(P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))$ and $\forall x\,(Q(x) \rightarrow R(x))$ using UG — a *direct* application of the workflow in Section 3.2.
- **Watch the variable hygiene.** Rename $c$ to $x$ in the final line; do not leave $\forall c$ in the conclusion.
- **UG is sound, not complete on its own.** Together with UI and propositional rules it yields a complete proof system for first-order logic, but UG alone is a one-way move.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Implications of Quantifiers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES (INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Implications of Quantifiers

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Quantifiers

In propositional and predicate logic, **quantifiers** are logical symbols that indicate the quantity (or scope) of the subject under consideration. The two principal quantifiers used throughout KTU Discrete Mathematical Structures are:

| Symbol | Name | Reading | Meaning |
|:------:|:----:|:-------:|:-------|
| $\forall$ | Universal Quantifier | "For all" or "For every" | Asserts that a predicate holds for **every** element in the domain of discourse. |
| $\exists$ | Existential Quantifier | "There exists" | Asserts that a predicate holds for **at least one** element in the domain of discourse. |

When quantifiers are combined with logical connectives such as $\rightarrow$, $\wedge$, $\vee$, and $\neg$, we obtain **quantified statements**. The **implications of quantifiers** refer to the resulting logical behaviours, equivalences, and transformations that occur when we manipulate statements of the form $\forall x\, P(x)$, $\exists x\, P(x)$, or nested combinations thereof.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 1 explicitly requires students to (i) translate English sentences into quantified logical form, (ii) determine the logical equivalence of quantified statements, and (iii) compute the negation of statements involving one or more quantifiers.

## 1.2 The Quantified Implication Statement

A **quantified implication** has the general canonical form:

$$\forall x \in D,\ P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)$$

Formally, this is read as: "For every element $x$ in the domain $D$, if $P(x)$ is true, then $Q(x)$ is true." Equivalently, it asserts that the set of elements satisfying $P$ is a **subset** of the set of elements satisfying $Q$ within the domain $D$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a classroom of **150 students** (the domain). The teacher makes two announcements:
>
> 1. *"**Every** student who submitted the assignment (P) will get full marks (Q)."* — This is a $\forall$ statement. To verify it, the teacher must inspect **all 150 records**. Even one counter-example falsifies the claim.
>
> 2. *"**Some** student who submitted the assignment (P) got distinction (Q)."* — This is an $\exists$ statement. To verify it, the teacher only needs to find **one** matching record. Even 149 failures do not falsify the claim.
>
> The **implication** $P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)$ within a quantifier is the conditional rule that links a property $P$ to a consequence $Q$ for each individual.

## 1.4 Why "Implications of Quantifiers" Matters

In real engineering domains, we constantly deal with quantified implications:

- **Software Verification:** "For every input $i$, if the input is valid ($P(i)$), then the program terminates ($Q(i)$)." This is a **universal conditional** — a foundational correctness statement.
- **Database Query Logic:** "There exists a record $r$ such that the salary exceeds the threshold and the department is active." — A nested existential conjunction.
- **Network Engineering:** "For all packets, if the packet is malformed, then the firewall drops it." — Universally quantified security policy.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics and Conventions Used

- **Domain of Discourse ($D$):** The non-empty set of all possible values a variable may take. Often $\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{N}$, $\mathbb{R}$, or a finite set.
- **Truth Value of a Quantified Statement:** $\forall x P(x)$ is **TRUE** iff $P(x)$ is true for **every** $x \in D$. $\exists x P(x)$ is **TRUE** iff $P(x)$ is true for **at least one** $x \in D$.
- **Empty Domain Convention (KTU Standard):** If the domain $D = \emptyset$, then $\forall x P(x)$ is vacuously **TRUE**, while $\exists x P(x)$ is **FALSE**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Logical Equivalences Involving Quantifiers

The manipulation of quantified statements rests on a small, but **high-yield**, set of equivalences. Every KTU examination question on this topic will test at least one of the rules below.

### Rule 1 — Negation of a Universal Statement

$$\neg \forall x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\, \neg P(x)$$

**Logic (Why):** To deny "every element satisfies $P$," it suffices to produce **one** element that violates $P$.

### Rule 2 — Negation of an Existential Statement

$$\neg \exists x\, P(x) \equiv \forall x\, \neg P(x)$$

**Logic (Why):** To deny "some element satisfies $P$," we must prove that **no** element satisfies $P$ — i.e., every element violates $P$.

### Rule 3 — Distribution of $\forall$ over $\wedge$

$$\forall x\, \big(P(x) \wedge Q(x)\big) \equiv \big(\forall x\, P(x)\big) \wedge \big(\forall x\, Q(x)\big)$$

### Rule 4 — Distribution of $\exists$ over $\vee$

$$\exists x\, \big(P(x) \vee Q(x)\big) \equiv \big(\exists x\, P(x)\big) \vee \big(\exists x\, Q(x)\big)$$

### Rule 5 — Non-Distribution Warnings (Most Common KTU Trap)

$$\forall x\, \big(P(x) \vee Q(x)\big) \not\equiv \big(\forall x\, P(x)\big) \vee \big(\forall x\, Q(x)\big)$$

$$\exists x\, \big(P(x) \wedge Q(x)\big) \not\equiv \big(\exists x\, P(x)\big) \wedge \big(\exists x\, Q(x)\big)$$

The implication arrows $\Rightarrow$ that **do** hold are:

$$\big(\forall x\, P(x)\big) \vee \big(\forall x\, Q(x)\big) \Rightarrow \forall x\, \big(P(x) \vee Q(x)\big)$$

$$\exists x\, \big(P(x) \wedge Q(x)\big) \Rightarrow \big(\exists x\, P(x)\big) \wedge \big(\exists x\, Q(x)\big)$$

### Rule 6 — Implication Conversion (Vacuous Truth)

$$P(x) \rightarrow Q(x) \equiv \neg P(x) \vee Q(x)$$

When this is placed inside a universal quantifier, the **vacuous truth principle** emerges: for any $x_0$ where $P(x_0)$ is **false**, the implication $P(x_0) \rightarrow Q(x_0)$ is automatically true, regardless of $Q(x_0)$.

$$\forall x\, \big(P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)\big) \equiv \forall x\, \big(\neg P(x) \vee Q(x)\big)$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Equivalence Sheet

| # | Law Name | Logical Statement | Engineering Use Case |
|:-:|:---------|:------------------|:--------------------|
| F1 | De Morgan for $\forall$ | $\neg \forall x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\, \neg P(x)$ | Finding counter-examples in software testing |
| F2 | De Morgan for $\exists$ | $\neg \exists x\, P(x) \equiv \forall x\, \neg P(x)$ | Proving non-existence in cryptography |
| F3 | $\forall$ over $\wedge$ | $\forall x\,(P \wedge Q) \equiv \forall x\, P \wedge \forall x\, Q$ | Multi-property system invariants |
| F4 | $\exists$ over $\vee$ | $\exists x\,(P \vee Q) \equiv \exists x\, P \vee \exists x\, Q$ | Error-handling branches (at least one fires) |
| F5 | $\forall$ over $\rightarrow$ | $\forall x\, P(x) \rightarrow \forall x\, Q(x)$ is **NOT** generally valid | Common KTU trap |
| F6 | Implication rewrite | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$ | Converting conditional specs to disjunctive form |
| F7 | Contrapositive (quantified) | $\forall x\,(P \rightarrow Q) \equiv \forall x\,(\neg Q \rightarrow \neg P)$ | Indirect proofs in algorithm correctness |
| F8 | Vacuous truth | $\forall x \in \emptyset,\ P(x)$ is **TRUE** | Boundary-condition proofs in empty lists |
| F9 | $\exists$ uniqueness | $\exists ! x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\, P(x) \wedge \forall y\,(P(y) \rightarrow y = x)$ | Database primary-key existence |
| F10 | $\exists$ over $\wedge$ (impl.) | $\exists x\,(P \wedge Q) \Rightarrow \exists x\, P \wedge \exists x\, Q$ | Witness extraction in proofs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Notice that the converse direction $\big(\exists x\, P(x) \wedge \exists x\, Q(x)\big) \Rightarrow \exists x\,(P(x) \wedge Q(x))$ is **FALSE**. Two witnesses may be **different** elements, so neither alone satisfies both $P$ and $Q$.

## 2.3 Nested Quantifiers — Order of Importance

When two quantifiers of **different types** are nested, their **order matters** and changes the meaning. The four nested forms and their KTU-standard readings are:

| Nested Form | Reading | Plain English |
|:-----------:|:-------:|:-------------|
| $\forall x\, \forall y\, P(x,y)$ | For all $x$ **and** for all $y$ | Every pair $(x,y)$ satisfies $P$. |
| $\exists x\, \exists y\, P(x,y)$ | There exists $x$ **and** there exists $y$ | Some pair $(x,y)$ satisfies $P$. |
| $\forall x\, \exists y\, P(x,y)$ | For every $x$, there exists a $y$ | For each $x$, we can find a (possibly $x$-dependent) $y$. |
| $\exists x\, \forall y\, P(x,y)$ | There exists an $x$ such that for every $y$ | A **single** $x$ works for **all** $y$. |

> [!NOTE]
> The last two are **not** equivalent: $\forall x\, \exists y\, P(x,y)$ does **not** imply $\exists x\, \forall y\, P(x,y)$. A KTU favourite!

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Software Verification:** A loop invariant is universally quantified over all iterations. Negating it yields a witness (test case) that breaks the invariant — used in model checking (e.g., CBMC, SPIN).
- **Database Query Optimisation:** SQL's `WHERE` clause with `EXISTS` and `FOR ALL` (via `NOT EXISTS`) directly maps to $\exists$ and $\neg \forall$. Query planners exploit distributive laws to rewrite queries.
- **Cryptographic Protocols:** "There does not exist an adversary who can forge a signature" — a $\neg \exists$ statement, often negated to $\forall \neg$ for proving security.
- **Compiler Design:** Type-checking rules often use $\forall \alpha$ (universally quantified type variables) to encode parametric polymorphism (generics in Java/C++).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of De Morgan's Law for Universal Quantifier

**Goal:** Prove $\neg \forall x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\, \neg P(x)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg \forall x\, P(x) &\equiv \neg \big(\text{“}P(x) \text{ is true for every } x \in D\text{”}\big) \\
&\equiv \text{“It is NOT the case that } P(x) \text{ is true for every } x\text{”} \\
&\equiv \text{“There is at least one } x \text{ for which } P(x) \text{ is false”} \\
&\equiv \text{“There exists an } x \text{ such that } \neg P(x) \text{ holds”} \\
&\equiv \exists x\, \neg P(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Step 1** unpacks the universal quantifier into its English meaning.
2. **Step 2** applies the outer negation, turning "for all" into a denial.
3. **Step 3** is the logical pivot: denying "all" is equivalent to affirming "some opposite."
4. **Step 4** rewrites the affirmation in the canonical existential form.
5. **Step 5** produces the final equivalence.

## 3.2 Derivation of De Morgan's Law for Existential Quantifier

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg \exists x\, P(x) &\equiv \neg \big(\text{“}P(x) \text{ is true for some } x \in D\text{”}\big) \\
&\equiv \text{“It is NOT the case that } P(x) \text{ is true for any } x\text{”} \\
&\equiv \text{“For every } x,\ P(x) \text{ is false”} \\
&\equiv \text{“For all } x,\ \neg P(x) \text{ holds”} \\
&\equiv \forall x\, \neg P(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Translating and Negating a Nested Statement

**Problem (KTU-Style):** Let $P(x)$ = "$x$ is a prime number greater than $2$" and $Q(x)$ = "$x$ is odd." Consider the universe $\mathbb{Z}^+$.

**(a)** Express in logical form: *"For every positive integer, if it is a prime greater than 2, then it is odd."*

**Solution:**

$$\forall x \in \mathbb{Z}^+,\ P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)$$

**(b)** Write the negation of this statement in plain English.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg \big(\forall x\, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x))\big) &\equiv \exists x\, \neg\big(P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)\big) \quad &\text{[De Morgan F1]} \\
&\equiv \exists x\, \big(\neg(\neg P(x) \vee Q(x))\big) \quad &\text{[Implication F6]} \\
&\equiv \exists x\, \big(P(x) \wedge \neg Q(x)\big) \quad &\text{[De Morgan on connectives]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plain English:** *"There exists a positive integer that is a prime greater than 2 and is **not** odd (i.e., is even)."*

**Verification:** $x = 2$ — but $2$ is not greater than $2$, so $P(2)$ is false. The only candidate that **could** falsify the original is some prime $>2$ that is even. Since the only even prime is $2$ itself, no such witness exists. Hence the negation is **false** and the original statement is **true** — consistent with the well-known fact that all primes greater than 2 are odd.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Order of Nested Quantifiers

**Problem:** Let $P(x,y)$ mean "$x < y$" over $\mathbb{N}$. Determine the truth values of:

**(i)** $\forall x\, \exists y\, P(x,y)$

**(ii)** $\exists x\, \forall y\, P(x,y)$

**Solution to (i):**

$$\forall x \in \mathbb{N},\ \exists y \in \mathbb{N} \text{ such that } x < y$$

For any $x$, we can pick $y = x + 1$. Hence the statement is **TRUE**.

**Solution to (ii):**

$$\exists x \in \mathbb{N} \text{ such that } \forall y \in \mathbb{N},\ x < y$$

This requires a **single** $x$ that is smaller than **every** $y \in \mathbb{N}$. But $\mathbb{N}$ contains $0$, and no positive integer is smaller than $0$. Hence the statement is **FALSE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Point:** This is the canonical KTU counter-example demonstrating that $\forall \exists$ and $\exists \forall$ are **not** interchangeable.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Transformation Pipeline for Negation

The table below captures the deterministic pipeline a student must follow when asked to negate a quantified statement:

| Step | Operation | Input | Output |
|:----:|:----------|:------|:-------|
| 1 | Identify the outermost quantifier type | $\forall$ or $\exists$ | Toggle to the opposite |
| 2 | Flip the quantifier symbol | $\forall \to \exists$, $\exists \to \forall$ | New quantified statement |
| 3 | Push negation inward to the predicate | $\neg P(x)$ | Negated atomic predicate |
| 4 | Convert $P \to Q$ | $P \to Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$ | Disjunctive form |
| 5 | Apply Boolean De Morgan inside | $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$ | Normal form |
| 6 | Re-translate to English | Logical expression | Plain-language statement |

## 3.6 Worked Example — Distributed Quantified Disjunction

**Problem:** Show that the statement $\big(\forall x\, P(x)\big) \vee \big(\forall x\, Q(x)\big)$ implies, but is **not** implied by, $\forall x\, (P(x) \vee Q(x))$.

**Proof of ($\Rightarrow$) direction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Assume } \forall x\, P(x). \text{ Then for any } x_0, P(x_0) \text{ is true, hence } P(x_0) \vee Q(x_0) \text{ is true.} \\
\text{Therefore } \forall x\, (P(x) \vee Q(x)) \text{ holds.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, if $\forall x\, Q(x)$ holds, the same conclusion follows by symmetry. Thus the left disjunct alone forces the universal disjunction.

**Counter-example to the converse ($\Leftarrow$ fails):**

Let $D = \{1, 2\}$, $P(1) = \text{true}$, $P(2) = \text{false}$, $Q(1) = \text{false}$, $Q(2) = \text{true}$.

Then $\forall x\, (P(x) \vee Q(x))$ is **TRUE** (each element satisfies at least one). But $\forall x\, P(x)$ is **FALSE** (since $P(2)$ is false), and $\forall x\, Q(x)$ is **FALSE** (since $Q(1)$ is false). Hence the disjunction $\big(\forall x\, P(x)\big) \vee \big(\forall x\, Q(x)\big)$ is also **FALSE**. This contradicts the converse, so the implication is **strictly one-way**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Negation of a Quantified Statement

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the deterministic decision process for negating any quantified logical statement encountered in a KTU examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Quantified Statement Received] --> b1{Identify Outermost Quantifier}
    b1 -- Universal Forall --> c1[Step 1: Replace forall with exists]
    b1 -- Existential Exists --> c2[Step 1: Replace exists with forall]
    c1 --> d1[Step 2: Negate Inner Predicate P of x]
    c2 --> d2[Step 2: Negate Inner Predicate P of x]
    d1 --> e1{Is Inner Formula Conditional?}
    d2 --> e2{Is Inner Formula Conditional?}
    e1 -- Yes --> f1[Apply P implies Q equivalent to not P or Q]
    e1 -- No --> g1[Push Negation through Connectives using De Morgan]
    e2 -- Yes --> f2[Apply P implies Q equivalent to not P or Q]
    e2 -- No --> g2[Push Negation through Connectives using De Morgan]
    f1 --> h1[Re translate to English Statement]
    f2 --> h2[Re translate to English Statement]
    g1 --> h1
    g2 --> h2
    h1 --> endA[Final Negated Logical Expression]
    h2 --> endA
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Quantifier Manipulation Engine

The diagram below depicts the **internal architecture** of a typical quantifier-handling subsystem as used in symbolic logic tools (e.g., computer algebra systems like Mathematica, proof assistants like Coq). It shows how raw quantified statements are received, parsed, and routed through transformation pipelines.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        a1[English Sentence]
    end
    subgraph Parser
        a1 --> b1[Lexical Tokenizer]
        b1 --> b2[Syntax Tree Builder]
    end
    subgraph Analyzer
        b2 --> c1[Quantifier Scope Detector]
        c1 --> c2[Predicate Extractor]
        c2 --> c3[Connective Classifier]
    end
    subgraph Transformer
        c3 --> d1{User Requested Operation}
        d1 -- Negate --> e1[De Morgan Quantifier Module]
        d1 -- Equivalence --> e2[Distribution Law Module]
        d1 -- Translate --> e3[English Renderer Module]
        e1 --> f1[Apply Negation Rules F1 and F2]
        e2 --> f2[Apply Distribution Laws F3 and F4]
        e3 --> f3[Apply English Template]
    end
    subgraph Output
        f1 --> g1[Negated Logical Form]
        f2 --> g2[Equivalent Logical Form]
        f3 --> g3[English Sentence]
    end
```

## 4.3 Quantifier Order Significance Map

The schematic below illustrates the **meaning gap** between the four nested-quantifier combinations, a frequently tested KTU concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[forall x exists y P of x,y] --> m1[Meaning: For each x, choose a y possibly depending on x]
    n2[exists x forall y P of x,y] --> m2[Meaning: One single x works uniformly for all y]
    n3[forall x forall y P of x,y] --> m3[Meaning: P holds for every pair x and y]
    n4[exists x exists y P of x,y] --> m4[Meaning: At least one specific pair x and y satisfies P]
    m1 --> k1[Implication: forall exists IMPLIES exists forall in symmetric domains only]
    m2 --> k2[Counterexample: P of x,y means x less than y over N: forall exists TRUE, exists forall FALSE]
    m3 --> k3[Symmetric: order does not matter for same quantifier type]
    m4 --> k4[Symmetric: order does not matter for same quantifier type]
```
