## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Structure of the Theorem

The Binomial Theorem operates on three pillars. Understanding **why** each works is critical for KTU exams:

- **Pillar 1 — Combinatorial Foundation:**
  When expanding $(x+y)^n = (x+y)(x+y)\cdots(x+y)$ ($n$ times), each term in the final expansion is formed by picking either $x$ or $y$ from each of the $n$ brackets. The number of ways to pick $y$ exactly $k$ times (and consequently $x$ exactly $n-k$ times) is the number of ways to choose $k$ positions out of $n$, which is $\binom{n}{k}$.

- **Pillar 2 — Algebraic Closure:**
  The exponent on $x$ decreases from $n$ down to $0$, while the exponent on $y$ increases from $0$ up to $n$. The sum of exponents is always $n$ in every term. This is the **Why** behind the $x^{n-k} y^k$ pattern.

- **Pillar 3 — Pascal's Recursive Engine:**
  Binomial coefficients are not computed independently — they are **recycled** through Pascal's Identity, which means a computer (or a student) only needs the base cases $\binom{n}{0}=\binom{n}{n}=1$ to generate every other coefficient.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table thoroughly** — these are the **high-yield formulas** that appear in nearly every KTU Module 2 question paper.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Engineering / Discrete Math Utility |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Binomial Theorem (General)** | $(x+y)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} y^k$ | Core expansion identity; used in algorithm analysis (e.g., polynomial time complexity). |
| 2 | **General / $(k+1)$-th Term** | $T_{k+1} = \binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} y^k$ | Used to find a specific term without full expansion. |
| 3 | **Binomial Coefficient** | $\binom{n}{k} = \dfrac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$ | Counts combinations; used in probability and counting. |
| 4 | **Pascal's Identity** | $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n-1}{k-1} + \binom{n-1}{k}$ | Recursive generation of coefficients; basis of Pascal's Triangle. |
| 5 | **Symmetry Property** | $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n}{n-k}$ | Reduces computation by half; useful in symmetric sums. |
| 6 | **Sum of Coefficients** | $\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} = 2^n$ | Found by setting $x = 1, y = 1$. Equals size of power set. |
| 7 | **Alternating Sum** | $\sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^k \binom{n}{k} = 0$ | Found by setting $x = 1, y = -1$. Used in inclusion-exclusion. |
| 8 | **Middle Term Index** | $\left(\dfrac{n}{2}+1\right)$-th term for even $n$ | Helps locate the central coefficient. |
| 9 | **Boundary Conditions** | $\binom{n}{0} = \binom{n}{n} = 1$ | Base cases for Pascal's Triangle. |
| 10 | **Constant Term Rule** | Term independent of $x$ is $\binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} y^k$ where $n-k = 0$ | Used in finding constant terms in algebraic expressions. |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Binomial Theorem is **not just algebra** — it has concrete applications in computer science and engineering:
> - **Algorithm Analysis:** The Master Theorem for recurrences uses binomial coefficient logic to expand $a^n + b^n$ forms.
> - **Probability & Statistics:** The Binomial Probability Distribution $P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ is built directly on this theorem.
> - **Cryptography:** Error-correcting codes (e.g., Hamming codes) use binomial coefficient sums.
> - **Digital Logic:** Counting Boolean functions of $n$ variables yields $2^{2^n}$ via repeated application.
> - **Network Theory:** Counting subnets in a binary tree structure.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Proof of the Binomial Theorem by Mathematical Induction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** The proof by **Mathematical Induction on $n$** is the most commonly asked derivation question worth **7 marks**.

**Theorem:** For every non-negative integer $n$,
$$(x+y)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} y^k$$

---

**Step 1 — Base Case ($n = 1$):**

Verify the formula holds for $n = 1$.

$$\text{LHS} = (x+y)^1 = x+y$$

$$\text{RHS} = \sum_{k=0}^{1} \binom{1}{k} x^{1-k} y^k = \binom{1}{0} x^1 y^0 + \binom{1}{1} x^0 y^1 = 1 \cdot x \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1 \cdot y = x+y$$

Since LHS = RHS, the base case holds. $\quad \blacksquare \text{(Base case verified)}$

---

**Step 2 — Inductive Hypothesis:**

Assume the theorem is true for some arbitrary non-negative integer $n = m$. That is, assume:

$$(x+y)^m = \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k} y^k$$

This is our **assumption** for the inductive step.

---

**Step 3 — Inductive Step (Prove for $n = m+1$):**

We must show that:

$$(x+y)^{m+1} = \sum_{k=0}^{m+1} \binom{m+1}{k} x^{(m+1)-k} y^k$$

**Starting Point:** Multiply both sides of the inductive hypothesis by $(x+y)$:

$$(x+y)^{m+1} = (x+y)^{m} \cdot (x+y)$$

$$= \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k} y^k \right] \cdot (x+y)$$

**Distribute** the multiplication:

$$= \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k} y^k \cdot x \; + \; \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k} y^k \cdot y$$

**Simplify** each term (multiply $x$ in the first, multiply $y$ in the second):

$$= \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k+1} y^k \; + \; \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k} y^{k+1}$$

**Re-index the second sum** by substituting $j = k+1$ (so $k = j-1$):

$$= \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k+1} y^k \; + \; \sum_{j=1}^{m+1} \binom{m}{j-1} x^{m-(j-1)} y^{j}$$

Renaming $j$ back to $k$ for uniformity:

$$= \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k+1} y^k \; + \; \sum_{k=1}^{m+1} \binom{m}{k-1} x^{m-k+1} y^{k}$$

**Extract the common $k=0$ and $k=m+1$ terms** explicitly:

**First sum, $k=0$ term:** $\binom{m}{0} x^{m+1} y^0 = 1 \cdot x^{m+1} = x^{m+1}$

**Second sum, $k=m+1$ term:** $\binom{m}{m} x^{0} y^{m+1} = 1 \cdot y^{m+1} = y^{m+1}$

**Combined middle range ($1 \le k \le m$):**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{m} \left[ \binom{m}{k} + \binom{m}{k-1} \right] x^{m+1-k} y^{k}$$

**Apply Pascal's Identity** (which we prove below in §3.2):

$$\binom{m}{k} + \binom{m}{k-1} = \binom{m+1}{k}$$

Substitute:

$$= x^{m+1} + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \binom{m+1}{k} x^{(m+1)-k} y^{k} + y^{m+1}$$

**Recognize** the three pieces — the first term is $k=0$ and the last term is $k=m+1$ in the final summation. Combine:

$$= \sum_{k=0}^{m+1} \binom{m+1}{k} x^{(m+1)-k} y^{k}$$

This is exactly the statement for $n = m+1$. $\quad \blacksquare \text{(Inductive step verified)}$

---

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

By the Principle of Mathematical Induction, the Binomial Theorem holds for **all** non-negative integers $n$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Proof of Pascal's Identity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Question:** Prove $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n-1}{k-1} + \binom{n-1}{k}$ using the factorial definition of binomial coefficients.

**Starting Definition:**

$$\binom{n-1}{k-1} + \binom{n-1}{k} = \frac{(n-1)!}{(k-1)!\,(n-k)!} + \frac{(n-1)!}{k!\,(n-1-k)!}$$

**Factor out** $\dfrac{(n-1)!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$ from both terms. Note that $(n-1-k)! = (n-k-1)!$.

For the first term, multiply and divide by $k$ to make the factorials match:

$$\frac{(n-1)!}{(k-1)!\,(n-k)!} = \frac{k \cdot (n-1)!}{k \cdot (k-1)!\,(n-k)!} = \frac{k \cdot (n-1)!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$$

For the second term, multiply and divide by $(n-k)$ to make the factorials match:

$$\frac{(n-1)!}{k!\,(n-1-k)!} = \frac{(n-k) \cdot (n-1)!}{k!\,(n-k)\cdot(n-1-k)!} = \frac{(n-k) \cdot (n-1)!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$$

**Combine** the two terms over the common denominator $k!\,(n-k)!$:

$$= \frac{k \cdot (n-1)!}{k!\,(n-k)!} + \frac{(n-k) \cdot (n-1)!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$$

$$= \frac{[k + (n-k)] \cdot (n-1)!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$$

$$= \frac{n \cdot (n-1)!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$$

**Recognize** that $n \cdot (n-1)! = n!$:

$$= \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!} = \binom{n}{k}$$

Thus, Pascal's Identity is proven. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Finding a Specific Term

**Problem:** Find the coefficient of $x^4$ in the expansion of $(2x + 3)^7$.

**Solution Strategy:** Use the general term formula.

**Step 1 — Write the General Term:**

$$T_{k+1} = \binom{7}{k} (2x)^{7-k} (3)^k$$

**Step 2 — Identify the exponent of $x$ in the general term:**

The power of $x$ in $T_{k+1}$ is $(7-k)$ since $(2x)^{7-k} = 2^{7-k} \cdot x^{7-k}$.

**Step 3 — Set the exponent equal to 4 and solve for $k$:**

$$7 - k = 4 \implies k = 3$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $k=3$ into the general term formula:**

$$T_4 = \binom{7}{3} (2x)^{4} (3)^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the coefficient and powers:**

$$\binom{7}{3} = \frac{7!}{3!\,4!} = \frac{7 \times 6 \times 5}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 35$$

$$(2x)^4 = 2^4 \cdot x^4 = 16 x^4$$

$$(3)^3 = 27$$

**Step 6 — Multiply all components:**

$$T_4 = 35 \times 16 \times 27 \times x^4 = 15120 \, x^4$$

**Final Answer:** The coefficient of $x^4$ is $\boxed{15120}$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pascal's Triangle — Binomial Coefficient Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram renders **Pascal's Triangle for $n = 0$ to $n = 5$**, showing how each internal coefficient is the sum of the two coefficients directly above it (Pascal's Identity in action).

```mermaid
graph TD
    N0["n EQUALS 0 : 1"]
    N1["n EQUALS 1 : 1 1"]
    N2["n EQUALS 2 : 1 2 1"]
    N3["n EQUALS 3 : 1 3 3 1"]
    N4["n EQUALS 4 : 1 4 6 4 1"]
    N5["n EQUALS 5 : 1 5 10 10 5 1"]
    N0 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N5
    N2L["C 2 1 EQUALS 2"]:::sumhighlight
    N3L["C 3 1 EQUALS 1 PLUS 2 EQUALS 3"]:::sumhighlight
    N3M["C 3 2 EQUALS 2 PLUS 1 EQUALS 3"]:::sumhighlight
    N4L["C 4 1 EQUALS 1 PLUS 3 EQUALS 4"]:::sumhighlight
    N4MM["C 4 2 EQUALS 3 PLUS 3 EQUALS 6"]:::sumhighlight
    N4R["C 4 3 EQUALS 3 PLUS 1 EQUALS 4"]:::sumhighlight
    N2 -.-> N2L
    N3 -.-> N3L
    N3 -.-> N3M
    N4 -.-> N4L
    N4 -.-> N4MM
    N4 -.-> N4R
    classDef sumhighlight fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Top to Bottom:** Each row $n$ gives the coefficients of $(x+y)^n$.
- **Symmetry:** Row $n$ is mirror-symmetric (e.g., $\binom{4}{1} = \binom{4}{3} = 4$).
- **Boundary Rule:** The leftmost and rightmost entries are always **1**.
- **Pascal's Recursion:** The highlighted (yellow) cells demonstrate Pascal's Identity — e.g., the central **6** in row 4 is $3 + 3$.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Binomial Expansion Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN["INPUT : Binomial X PLUS Y and Power N"]:::input
    BASE["Base Case Check : N EQUALS 0 ?"]:::process
    RETBASE["Return 1"]:::output
    COEFF["Compute Coefficient : N CHOOSE K for each K from 0 to N"]:::process
    POWX["Compute X Power : N MINUS K"]:::process
    POWY["Compute Y Power : K"]:::process
    TERM["Form Term : COEFF times X POW X times Y POW Y"]:::process
    SUM["Sum All Terms"]:::process
    OUT["OUTPUT : Full Polynomial Expansion"]:::output
    IN --> BASE
    BASE -- YES --> RETBASE --> OUT
    BASE -- NO --> COEFF --> POWX --> POWY --> TERM --> SUM --> OUT
    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- This block-level flow shows how a **mathematical engine** would expand a binomial programmatically.
- For a *human exam*, you follow the same pipeline **by hand** when the question says *"expand $(x+y)^n$"*.
