A_0, A_1, ..., A_n-1"] OUT --> AP1["Application Signal Filtering
Keep only low frequency A_j"] OUT --> AP2["Application Spectrum Analysis
Plot |A_j| versus j"] OUT --> AP3["Application Inverse Transform
Recover time domain signal"] AP3 --> REC["Reconstructed Sequence
a_0, a_1, ..., a_n-1"] style DFT fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Algorithm Pipeline for Evaluating Sums via Roots of Unity ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1 Identify sum of form
sum P of k times omega to the kr"] --> S2["Step 2 Take DFT of sequence P of k
A_j equals sum P of k omega to the jk"] S2 --> S3["Step 3 Multiply by master sum factor
use sum omega to the j minus k times r equals n if j equals r else 0"] S3 --> S4["Step 4 Pick out surviving term
only j equals r contributes"] S4 --> S5["Final Answer equals n times A_r
provided r is between 0 and n-1"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** State and prove Euler's formula. Hence find the principal $4$-th root of unity. **Model Answer:** Euler's formula states that for any real $\theta$: $$e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$$ *Proof (sketch):* Differentiate both sides of $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$ with respect to $\theta$. Both sides satisfy $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} = iy$ with $y(0)=1$, so by uniqueness of ODE solutions they are equal. Principal $4$-th root: set $\theta = 2\pi/4 = \pi/2$: $$\omega_4 = e^{i\pi/2} = \cos(\pi/2) + i\sin(\pi/2) = 0 + i\cdot 1 = i$$ #### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Using the orthogonality of roots of unity, prove that $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \omega^{kr} = \begin{cases} n & \text{if } n \mid r \\ 0 & \text{otherwise}\end{cases}$ where $\omega = e^{2\pi i/n}$. **Model Answer:** Let $\omega = e^{2\pi i/n}$, so $\omega^n = 1$. - If $n \mid r$, then $\omega^r = 1$, hence every term $\omega^{kr} = 1$ and the sum equals $n$. - If $n \nmid r$, then $\omega^r \ne 1$ and the sum is geometric: $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^{kr} = \frac{(\omega^r)^n - 1}{\omega^r - 1} = \frac{\omega^{rn}-1}{\omega^r - 1} = \frac{1-1}{\omega^r - 1} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$ ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Find all the $5$-th roots of unity. Plot them on the complex plane and state their geometric properties. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{4}\sum_{j=0}^{4}\omega^{k+j}$ where $\omega = e^{2\pi i/5}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** The $5$-th roots of unity are solutions to $x^5 = 1$, given by $\omega^k = e^{2\pi i k/5}$ for $k = 0,1,2,3,4$. Explicitly: - $\omega^0 = 1$ - $\omega^1 = \cos(72°) + i\sin(72°) \approx 0.309 + 0.951i$ - $\omega^2 = \cos(144°) + i\sin(144°) \approx -0.809 + 0.588i$ - $\omega^3 = \cos(216°) + i\sin(216°) \approx -0.809 - 0.588i$ - $\omega^4 = \cos(288°) + i\sin(288°) \approx 0.309 - 0.951i$ Geometric properties: - All five roots lie on the unit circle $|\omega^k| = 1$. - They form the vertices of a regular pentagon inscribed in the unit circle, centered at the origin. - The arguments are equally spaced at $72° = 2\pi/5$ apart. - They are closed under multiplication: $\omega^a \cdot \omega^b = \omega^{(a+b)\bmod 5}$, forming a cyclic group of order 5. - Complex conjugation: $\overline{\omega^k} = \omega^{5-k}$. **(b)** The double sum factors as a product of two single sums: $$\sum_{k=0}^{4}\sum_{j=0}^{4}\omega^{k+j} = \left(\sum_{k=0}^{4}\omega^k\right)\left(\sum_{j=0}^{4}\omega^j\right)$$ Each single sum is a geometric series with $r = \omega$: $$\sum_{k=0}^{4}\omega^k = \frac{\omega^5 - 1}{\omega - 1} = \frac{1 - 1}{\omega - 1} = 0$$ (since $\omega^5 = 1$ and $\omega \ne 1$). Hence the double sum equals $0 \cdot 0 = 0$. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of a sequence $\{a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}\}$. Write down its inverse and verify the inversion formula for $n = 2$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Use the DFT technique to evaluate $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k\omega^{kr}$ where $\omega = e^{2\pi i/n}$ and $r$ is a non-zero integer modulo $n$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** The DFT of a sequence $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}$ is the sequence $A_0, A_1, \dots, A_{n-1}$: $$A_j = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a_k\, \omega_n^{jk}, \quad \omega_n = e^{2\pi i/n}$$ The inverse DFT is: $$a_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} A_j\, \omega_n^{-jk}$$ **Verification for $n = 2$:** Here $\omega_2 = e^{i\pi} = -1$. Forward DFT: $A_0 = a_0\cdot 1 + a_1\cdot 1 = a_0 + a_1$ $A_1 = a_0\cdot 1 + a_1\cdot (-1) = a_0 - a_1$ Inverse DFT: $\frac{1}{2}[A_0 \cdot 1 + A_1 \cdot 1] = \frac{1}{2}[(a_0+a_1) + (a_0 - a_1)] = \frac{1}{2}[2a_0] = a_0$ ✓ $\frac{1}{2}[A_0 \cdot 1 + A_1 \cdot (-1)] = \frac{1}{2}[(a_0+a_1) - (a_0 - a_1)] = \frac{1}{2}[2a_1] = a_1$ ✓ **(b)** Define $S = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k\omega^{kr}$. We compute the DFT of the sequence $a_k = k$ at frequency $r$: $$A_r = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k\,\omega^{kr}$$ We will derive this using the trick of differentiating the geometric series. Recall the geometric sum: $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\omega^{kr} = \frac{\omega^{rn} - 1}{\omega^r - 1} = \frac{1 - 1}{\omega^r - 1} = 0 \quad \text{(since } n \nmid r\text{)}$$ Differentiate both sides with respect to $\omega^r$ (treating $\omega^r$ as variable, but with respect to the common ratio $x = \omega^r$): $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k\, x^{k-1} = \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{x^n - 1}{x - 1}\right]$$ Multiplying both sides by $x$ and using quotient rule: $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k\,x^{k} = x \cdot \frac{nx^{n-1}(x-1) - (x^n - 1)}{(x-1)^2}$$ Setting $x = \omega^r$ where $\omega^{rn} = 1$: $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k\,\omega^{kr} = \omega^r \cdot \frac{0\cdot(\omega^r - 1) - (1 - 1)}{(\omega^r - 1)^2} = \omega^r \cdot \frac{0}{(\omega^r-1)^2} = 0$$ Wait — both numerator terms vanish! We need a more careful derivation. The numerator: $nx^{n-1}(x-1) - (x^n - 1) = nx^n - nx^{n-1} - x^n + 1 = (n-1)x^n - nx^{n-1} + 1$. At $x = \omega^r$, $x^n = 1$, so this becomes $(n-1) - n\omega^{-r} + 1 = n - n\omega^{-r} = n(1 - \omega^{-r})$. Hence: $$S = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k\omega^{kr} = \omega^r \cdot \frac{n(1-\omega^{-r})}{(\omega^r - 1)^2} = \frac{n\omega^r(1-\omega^{-r})}{(\omega^r - 1)^2}$$ Simplify numerator: $n(\omega^r - 1)$. Denominator: $(\omega^r - 1)^2$. Therefore: $$\boxed{S = \frac{n\omega^r}{\omega^r - 1}}$$ This is the closed-form expression. Note this is valid only when $n \nmid r$ (otherwise the denominator $\omega^r - 1 = 0$ and we get a different case, requiring a limit). For $r = 0$: $S = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$, which can be obtained as the limit of $\frac{n\omega^r}{\omega^r - 1}$ as $r \to 0$ (using L'Hôpital or Taylor expansion). $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - **Pitfall 1:** Students frequently forget to apply the condition $n \mid r$ in the master sum. The two cases ($n \mid r$ and $n \nmid r$) yield *completely different* answers ($n$ vs $0$). Always state the condition explicitly. > - **Pitfall 2:** In the DFT, the *forward* transform uses $\omega^{jk}$ while the *inverse* uses $\omega^{-jk}$ AND has a $\frac{1}{n}$ factor. Mixing these up costs full marks. > - **Pitfall 3:** When evaluating $\sum k\omega^{kr}$, do **not** blindly quote the master sum. Differentiation of the geometric series is required. Writing the wrong formula loses 3–5 marks. > - **Pitfall 4:** Plotting roots of unity: students often forget to label angles $2\pi k/n$ or omit the unit circle. Always draw a unit circle for clarity. > - **Pitfall 5:** Conjugate property: $\overline{\omega^k} = \omega^{-k} = \omega^{n-k}$. The middle equality follows because $\omega^n = 1$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Euler's formula** $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$ is the single most important identity in this module — derive or state it correctly. - The **$n$-th roots of unity** are $\omega^k = e^{2\pi i k/n}$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, n-1$; they form a regular $n$-gon on the unit circle. - **Master sum** (orthogonality): $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \omega^{kr} = \begin{cases} n & n \mid r \\ 0 & n \nmid r \end{cases}$$ - **DFT forward:** $A_j = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a_k \omega^{jk}$. - **DFT inverse:** $a_k = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} A_j \omega^{-jk}$. - The DFT matrix $F$ with $F_{jk} = \omega^{jk}$ satisfies $F \cdot \overline{F} = n I$. - **Conjugate property:** $\overline{\omega^k} = \omega^{-k} = \omega^{n-k}$. - **Sum $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k \omega^{kr} = \dfrac{n\omega^r}{\omega^r - 1}$** when $n \nmid r$ (memorize for Part B derivations). - **Polynomial sums:** Use the binomial basis $k^r = \sum \binom{k}{j} \cdot S(r,j)$ (Stirling numbers of the second kind) to reduce to hockey-stick identities. - **Finite difference operator:** $\Delta f(k) = f(k+1) - f(k)$; iterates give Taylor-like expansion in falling factorials. - **Real-world KTU linkage:** DFT/FFT appears in signal processing (sampling theorem), cryptography (NTT-based schemes), and counting necklaces (Burnside's lemma + DFT). - **Geometric insight:** Always visualize roots of unity as points on the unit circle — this prevents sign errors in exams. - **Always check** whether $r = 0$ separately before applying DFT-based sum formulas.

topics.title: Exponential Generating Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT205 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES (INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exponential Generating Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

Let $(a_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be a sequence of real (or complex) numbers. The **Exponential Generating Function (EGF)** of the sequence $\{a_0, a_1, a_2, \dots\}$ is defined as the formal power series:

$$E(x) \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n \, \frac{x^n}{n!} \;=\; a_0 + a_1 \, \frac{x}{1!} + a_2 \, \frac{x^2}{2!} + a_3 \, \frac{x^3}{3!} + \cdots$$

The quantity $\dfrac{x^n}{n!}$ is called the **exponential monomial**, and $n!$ acts as a normalizing denominator. The coefficient $a_n$ is recovered using the **coefficient extraction operator** $[x^n]E(x)$:

$$a_n \;=\; n! \, [x^n] \, E(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Highlight — Distinction from Ordinary Generating Functions (OGF)**
> An **Ordinary GF** uses monomials $x^n$, while an **Exponential GF** uses monomials $\dfrac{x^n}{n!}$. EGFs are the natural tool when we are counting **labeled** structures (permutations, set partitions, derangements, labelled graphs) because the $n!$ factor cancels combinatorial over-counting due to label permutations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Labeled Envelope"

Imagine each object in a counting problem carries a **unique name tag** (label) — say, person A, B, C, $\dots$ When we count arrangements of $n$ labeled objects, the order/identity of labels matters. For instance, the number of ways to arrange a labeled committee of $n$ people is $n!$ — not just $1$.

An **OGF** is like stuffing all arrangements into identical envelopes (it cannot distinguish labels easily). An **EGF**, on the other hand, attaches a **unique stamped seal** for each label, allowing us to recover exact counts via $n! \, [x^n]$. That is why EGFs are the natural choice whenever the question involves **permutations, distributions among labeled boxes, or structure on labeled sets**.

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Engineering Connection**
> EGFs are foundational in:
> - **Algorithm analysis**: counting labeled trees, labeled graphs, permutations.
> - **Network design**: counting ways to label $n$ nodes of a network.
> - **Reliability engineering**: counting distinct failure label assignments.
> - **Compiler design**: counting labeled ordered trees (parse trees).

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need EGFs?

| Counting Scenario | Correct Tool |
|---|---|
| Number of subsets of an $n$-element set | OGF |
| Number of **labeled** structures on $n$ elements | **EGF** |
| Number of compositions, partitions | OGF |
| Number of permutations, derangements, matchings | **EGF** |

---

## 1.4 Notation Convention Used Throughout

- $E(x)$ — the EGF as a formal series.
- $a_n = n! \, [x^n] E(x)$ — coefficient extraction.
- $E_a(x)$, $E_b(x)$ — EGFs of sequences $\{a_n\}$ and $\{b_n\}$.
- $\binom{n}{k}$ — standard binomial coefficient (read as "$n$ choose $k$").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Theory

### Step 1 — Sequence → EGF (Encoding)
Given $\{a_n\}$, replace each $x^n$ by $\dfrac{x^n}{n!}$. The resulting formal series encodes the entire sequence.

### Step 2 — Algebraic Manipulation (Operational Closure)
EGFs behave like ordinary functions under addition, scalar multiplication, differentiation, integration, and convolution.

### Step 3 — Coefficient Extraction (Decoding)
Extract the $n^{th}$ coefficient using the formula $a_n = n! \, [x^n] E(x)$. This recovers the original combinatorial count.

---

## 2.2 The Big Theorem — Product of Two EGFs

If $E_a(x) \cdot E_b(x) = E_c(x)$, then:

$$c_n \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} \, a_k \, b_{n-k}$$

This is the **EGF convolution rule**, often called the **binomial convolution**. It is the EGF analogue of the Cauchy product for OGFs, but with a binomial coefficient instead of a plain product.

### Why does this matter?
Whenever we **split** a labeled set of $n$ elements into two labeled blocks of sizes $k$ and $n-k$, the number of ways to do that is $\binom{n}{k}$. So the product rule automatically accounts for all label-distribution possibilities.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Sequence $\{a_n\}$ | Exponential Generating Function $E(x)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_n = 1$ (constant sequence) | $e^{x}$ |
| 2 | $a_n = n$ | $x \, e^{x}$ |
| 3 | $a_n = n^2$ | $(x^2 + x) \, e^{x}$ |
| 4 | $a_n = \binom{n}{k}$ (fixed $k$) | $\dfrac{x^k}{k!} \, e^{x}$ |
| 5 | $a_n = n!$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-x}$ |
| 6 | $a_n = \binom{m}{n}$ | $(1+x)^m$ |
| 7 | $a_n = P(n,k)$ (permutations) | $\dfrac{x^k}{(1-x)^{k+1}}$ |
| 8 | $a_n = 2^n$ | $e^{2x}$ |
| 9 | $a_n = c^n$ (constant $c$) | $e^{cx}$ |
| 10 | $a_n = n! \, \binom{m}{n}$ | $(1+x)^m$ (after $n!$-cancelation) |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Mnemonic**
> "$e^{cx}$" is the EGF of $c^n$. Just remember: **$e^{x}$ is the universal EGF for the constant sequence**, and every constant sequence variant comes from $e^{cx}$.

---

## 2.4 Closed-Form Shortcuts

| Operation on $\{a_n\}$ | Effect on $E(x)$ |
|---|---|
| Multiply $a_n$ by $c$ | $c \cdot E(x)$ |
| Shift: $b_n = a_{n+1}$ | $E'(x)$ |
| Sum: $b_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} a_k$ | $\dfrac{E(x)}{1-x}$ |
| Binomial shift: $b_n = a_{n+k}$ | $\dfrac{d^k}{dx^k} E(x)$ |
| Convolution (binomial) | Product $E_a(x) E_b(x)$ |

---

## 2.5 Standard EGFs to Memorize for KTU

$$
e^{x} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!}, \qquad
\frac{1}{1-x} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n! \, \frac{x^n}{n!}, \qquad
(1+x)^m = \sum_{n=0}^{m} \binom{m}{n} \, x^n
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - In **compiler design**, the EGF $(1-x)^{-1}$ counts labeled linear orders (permutations), fundamental in scheduling algorithms.
> - In **network topology analysis**, $e^x$ counts the number of labeled disconnected graphs on $n$ vertices (via the exponential formula).
> - In **queueing theory**, EGFs encode service-time distributions and arrival sequences.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — EGF of the Constant Sequence $a_n = 1$

**Given:** $a_n = 1$ for all $n \ge 0$.

**Step 1.** Substitute $a_n = 1$ into the defining series:

$$E(x) \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 1 \cdot \frac{x^n}{n!} \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!}$$

**Step 2.** Recognize the Taylor series of the exponential function:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!} \;=\; e^{x}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\,\text{EGF of } \{1,1,1,\dots\} \text{ is } e^{x}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — EGF of the Linear Sequence $a_n = n$

**Given:** $a_n = n$.

**Step 1.** Write the series:

$$E(x) \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot \frac{x^n}{n!}$$

**Step 2.** Simplify using $n \cdot \dfrac{1}{n!} = \dfrac{1}{(n-1)!}$ for $n \ge 1$:

$$E(x) \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{(n-1)!} \;=\; x \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{x^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $m = n - 1$:

$$E(x) \;=\; x \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^m}{m!} \;=\; x \, e^{x}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\,\text{EGF of } \{0, 1, 2, 3, \dots\} \text{ is } x e^{x}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — EGF of $a_n = n^2$

**Step 1.** Decompose $n^2 = n(n-1) + n$:

$$E(x) \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n^2 \frac{x^n}{n!} \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n(n-1) \frac{x^n}{n!} + \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \frac{x^n}{n!}$$

**Step 2.** First sum: $n(n-1) \cdot \dfrac{x^n}{n!} = \dfrac{x^n}{(n-2)!}$, so:

$$\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{(n-2)!} \;=\; x^2 \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^m}{m!} \;=\; x^2 e^{x}$$

**Step 3.** Second sum (already derived) is $x e^{x}$.

**Conclusion:**

$$E(x) \;=\; x^2 e^{x} + x e^{x} \;=\; (x^2 + x) \, e^{x}$$

---

## 3.4 Proof of the Product Rule (Binomial Convolution Theorem)

**Statement:** If $E_a(x) \cdot E_b(x) = E_c(x)$, then $c_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} a_k b_{n-k}$.

**Step 1.** Multiply:

$$E_a(x) \, E_b(x) \;=\; \left( \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a_k \frac{x^k}{k!} \right) \! \left( \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} b_m \frac{x^m}{m!} \right)$$

**Step 2.** Expand the product (Cauchy rule):

$$E_a E_b \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} a_k b_m \frac{x^{k+m}}{k! \, m!}$$

**Step 3.** Re-index using $n = k + m$:

$$E_a E_b \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( \sum_{k=0}^{n} a_k b_{n-k} \frac{1}{k! \, (n-k)!} \right) x^n$$

**Step 4.** Recognize $\dfrac{1}{k!(n-k)!} = \dfrac{1}{n!} \binom{n}{k}$:

$$E_a E_b \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( \frac{1}{n!} \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} a_k b_{n-k} \right) x^n$$

**Step 5.** Extract the coefficient of $\dfrac{x^n}{n!}$:

$$c_n \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} a_k b_{n-k}$$

$$\boxed{\,c_n \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} a_k b_{n-k}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked-Out Mini Examples

| Sequence | EGF Setup | Final EGF | Coefficient Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| $a_n = 3^n$ | $\sum 3^n \frac{x^n}{n!}$ | $e^{3x}$ | $a_2 = 9 = 2! \cdot [x^2] e^{3x}$ ✓ |
| $a_n = n!$ | $\sum n! \frac{x^n}{n!}$ | $\frac{1}{1-x}$ | $a_3 = 6 = 3! \cdot [x^3] (1-x)^{-1}$ ✓ |
| $a_n = \binom{4}{n}$ | $\sum \binom{4}{n} x^n$ | $(1+x)^4$ | $a_2 = 6$ ✓ |
| $a_n = n + 2^n$ | $\sum (n + 2^n) \frac{x^n}{n!}$ | $x e^{x} + e^{2x}$ | $a_3 = 3 + 8 = 11$ ✓ |

---

## 3.6 Full Worked Example — Find EGF of $a_n = n! \cdot \binom{m}{n}$

**Step 1.** Recall $\binom{m}{n} = \dfrac{m!}{n!(m-n)!}$. So $a_n = n! \cdot \binom{m}{n} = \dfrac{m!}{(m-n)!}$.

**Step 2.** Write the EGF:

$$E(x) \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{m} \frac{m!}{(m-n)!} \cdot \frac{x^n}{n!}$$

**Step 3.** Re-index with $k = m - n$:

$$E(x) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{m} \frac{m!}{k!} \cdot \frac{x^{m-k}}{(m-k)!} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{m-k}$$

**Step 4.** Recognize this as $x^m \sum_{k=0}^{m} \binom{m}{k} x^{-k} \cdot \text{(careful)}$. Direct summation gives:

$$E(x) \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{m} \binom{m}{j} x^j \;=\; (1+x)^m$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{E(x) = (1+x)^m}$.

This is a critical KTU result: the EGF of the **binomial sequence** $\binom{m}{n}$ is $(1+x)^m$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EGF Workflow

The diagram below depicts the entire operational pipeline for working with Exponential Generating Functions, from raw sequence input to recovered coefficient.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
